questions.stem: What does the Actualization phase consist of, according to the lab definition?

options.text: Locating the concept, analysing the impact set, and prefactoring the code
Initiation of the change request, verification, and conclusion
Implementation of the new functionality, its incorporation into the old code, and change propagation
Postfactoring the new code, testing it, and releasing it

questions.explanation: The lab defines actualization as implement + incorporate + propagate: 'the implementation of the new functionality, its incorporation into the old code, and change propagation that seeks out and updates all places in the old code that require secondary modification.' A lists earlier phases (Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring). B lists the surrounding process steps, not actualization's content. D describes later/parallel activities. [ActLab]

questions.stem: In Rajlich's change process, where does Actualization sit relative to the other phases?

options.text: After Prefactoring and before Postfactoring
Before Impact Analysis and after Concept Location
After Conclusion and before Verification
Before Concept Location and after Initiation

questions.explanation: The process is Initiation -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Conclusion, all under Verification. Actualization therefore follows Prefactoring and precedes Postfactoring. D, C, and B place it before phases that actually precede it (Concept Location, Impact Analysis) or after Conclusion, which is the last phase. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: According to the Actualization deck, what is the one-line job of the Actualization phase?

options.text: Programmers estimate which classes the change will impact
Programmers remove all duplicated code before the change
Programmers locate the class where the concept first appears
Programmers implement the new functionality according to the change request

questions.explanation: The deck states Actualization is where programmers 'implement the new functionality according to the change request.' C is Concept Location, A is Impact Analysis, and B is a refactoring activity. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: The Actualization deck says the process of actualization varies primarily with what?

options.text: The programming language used
The age of the codebase
The size of the change
The number of programmers on the team

questions.explanation: The central organising idea of the deck is that 'the process varies with the size of the change' — small changes are edited directly; larger changes are written separately and incorporated. Codebase age, team size, and language are not the deck's organising variable. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: How are SMALL changes handled during actualization, per the deck?

options.text: Implemented only after a full impact-set re-analysis
Written as new classes in isolation and then incorporated
Edited directly into the existing old code
Deferred to the Postfactoring phase

questions.explanation: Small changes are 'done directly in the old code' — the deck's example is widening a ZIP field inside the existing Address class. B describes the technique for LARGER changes. D and A invent steps not tied to change size. (Actualization p.2-4)

questions.stem: Which running example does the deck use to illustrate a SMALL change edited directly into old code?

options.text: Adding a Cashiers class to the Point-of-Sale model
Replacing an obsolete class and deleting it
Adding a Pig subclass to a Farm of FarmAnimals
Widening an Address ZIP field from zip[5] to zip[9]

questions.explanation: The small-change example is the Address class whose ZIP field is widened from 5-digit (zip[5]) to 9-digit (zip[9]) — a localized edit inside one class with no new structure. The Pig/Farm example illustrates polymorphic extension; Cashiers illustrates incorporation of a new supplier; replacement/deletion is a separate operation. (Actualization p.2-4)

questions.stem: How are LARGER changes handled during actualization, per the deck?

options.text: The change is split into small ZIP-style edits across many classes
Old code is edited line by line until the new behaviour appears
The whole module is rewritten from scratch to avoid touching old code
New classes are implemented separately from the old code, then plugged in (incorporated), and the change may ripple

questions.explanation: For larger changes the deck says: 'Programmers implement the new classes separately from the old code... The new code is plugged into the existing code (incorporation). The change can propagate to other components (ripple effect).' C (rewrite) and B (direct edit, the SMALL-change technique) are wrong; A mischaracterises a large change as a bundle of trivial edits. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: Why does the deck recommend writing the new classes SEPARATELY from the old code before incorporating them?

options.text: Because separate classes are exempt from change propagation
So they can be made correct and testable in isolation before wiring them in, after which incorporation begins the ripple
Because the compiler forbids editing two files in the same change
So the impact set can be recomputed from scratch each time

questions.explanation: Building new classes apart from old code lets you get them right and testable before connecting them; only at incorporation do ripple effects begin. C is a fabricated compiler rule. D confuses this with Impact Analysis. A is false — incorporated classes are exactly what triggers propagation. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: Which phase immediately PRECEDES Actualization and prepares a clean insertion point for the new code?

options.text: Conclusion
Postfactoring
Concept Location
Prefactoring

questions.explanation: Prefactoring precedes Actualization and cleans an insertion seam so the new code has a clean place to be plugged in. Postfactoring and Conclusion follow Actualization; Concept Location occurs earlier (it finds the starting class). (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: Which phase immediately FOLLOWS Actualization to clean up the now-working code?

options.text: Prefactoring
Postfactoring
Impact Analysis
Initiation

questions.explanation: Actualization is followed by Postfactoring (clean up the working code) and then Conclusion, all under Verification. Prefactoring, Impact Analysis, and Initiation all precede Actualization. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: The predicted impact set produced by Impact Analysis corresponds, during Actualization, to what?

options.text: The set of classes that can never change
The set of obsolete classes to be deleted
The set of classes you now expect to touch during incorporation and propagation
The list of test cases that must be written first

questions.explanation: Impact Analysis's predicted impact set is exactly the set of classes you expect to touch during incorporation and propagation; actualization is where that prediction is tested. A inverts it, D confuses it with a test plan, and B narrows it to deletions only. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: A change request widens one field of a single class and adds no new structure. Which actualization technique applies?

options.text: Replace the class entirely and delete the obsolete one
Add a polymorphic subclass to extend a composite responsibility
Edit the change directly into the existing old code (small change)
Write a new class in isolation and incorporate it as a new supplier

questions.explanation: Widening one field of one class with no new structure is the canonical SMALL change (the zip[5]->zip[9] example) — edit it directly. D, B, and A are larger-change techniques (new supplier, polymorphic extension, replacement) that introduce new structure this scenario does not need. (Actualization p.2-5)

questions.stem: Why does the deck's claim that 'actualization varies with the size of the change' mirror the broader change process?

options.text: Because the size of a change is fixed before Initiation and cannot be re-evaluated
Because small changes skip Verification while large changes require it
Because prefactoring prepares a clean insertion point that actualization then fills, so the technique chosen depends on how much new structure must be inserted
Because every change, large or small, requires exactly the same incorporation steps

questions.explanation: The deck links size to technique: prefactoring (Lecture 4) prepared a clean seam, and actualization fills it — a small edit needs no new structure, a large change needs new classes incorporated into the prepared seam. D is false (small changes need no incorporation). A is false (size is judged during actualization). B is false — Verification wraps the entire process regardless of size. (Actualization p.1, p.5)

questions.stem: What is 'incorporation' in the actualization phase?

options.text: Plugging newly written classes into the existing system as components so the old code uses them
Removing duplicated code before the change is made
Estimating which classes a change will affect
Computing precision and recall of the impact prediction

questions.explanation: Incorporation is the act of plugging newly written classes into the existing code as components: 'New classes are plugged as components into the appropriate place of the existing code.' C is Impact Analysis, B is refactoring, D is the precision/recall scoring done after propagation. (Actualization p.8)

questions.stem: When new classes are incorporated, what do they assume, per the deck?

options.text: Ownership of the entire database schema
The role of the system's verification harness
The responsibilities demanded by the change request
The position of the innermost Clean Architecture layer

questions.explanation: The deck states the new classes 'assume the responsibilities demanded by the change request.' A, B, and D are unrelated roles not assigned by incorporation. (Actualization p.8)

questions.stem: The deck shows three structural shapes of incorporation. Which list is correct?

options.text: Singleton; Observer; Factory
True positive; false positive; false negative
Entities; Use Cases; Interface Adapters
New responsibility local; new responsibility composite; incorporating a new supplier

questions.explanation: The three incorporation shapes are: new responsibility local (p.9), new responsibility composite (p.10), and incorporating a new supplier (p.11). A lists design patterns, C lists Clean Architecture layers, and B lists confusion-matrix categories. (Actualization p.9-11)

questions.stem: In the 'new responsibility is LOCAL' shape of incorporation, what is the structure?

options.text: An old class is replaced and then deleted
The new code is a self-contained leaf: old code calls into it, but nothing new calls back out
The new class becomes a supplier that many existing clients now point to
The new code depends on a small subtree of further components

questions.explanation: The 'local' shape is the simplest: a self-contained leaf that old code calls into, with no new outward dependencies. D describes the COMPOSITE shape, C describes the NEW SUPPLIER shape, and A is replacement/deletion. (Actualization p.9)

questions.stem: In the 'new responsibility is COMPOSITE' shape of incorporation, what makes it different from the local shape?

options.text: It deletes an obsolete class as a side effect
The new code itself depends on / is composed of further components, so incorporating it brings a small subtree
The new code has no dependencies at all
It requires recomputing precision and recall

questions.explanation: In the composite shape the new code depends on further components, so plugging it in brings a small subtree rather than a single node. C describes the local (leaf) shape. A is deletion, D is impact scoring. (Actualization p.10)

questions.stem: In which incorporation shape is change propagation MOST likely, and why?

options.text: New responsibility composite, because subtrees never ripple
Incorporating a new supplier, because every existing client of the new supplier may need adjustment
Replacement, because references are never rerouted
New responsibility local, because the leaf has no dependents

questions.explanation: Incorporating a new supplier means existing client classes now point at the new class, so every client may need adjusting — this is where propagation is most likely. The local shape is the simplest (least rippling); the composite-subtree claim 'never ripple' is false; replacement explicitly reroutes references. (Actualization p.11)

questions.stem: What does 'replacement of a class' involve during actualization?

options.text: A new class takes over an old class's role, all references are rerouted, and deleting the obsolete class triggers its own propagation
Two classes are merged into one without changing any references
An interface is split into several client-specific interfaces
A field is widened inside a single existing class

questions.explanation: Replacement means a new class takes over the old class's role, all references must be rerouted, and deleting the now-obsolete class then triggers its own change propagation. B, C, and D describe merging, ISP segregation, and a small ZIP-style edit respectively. (Actualization p.15)

questions.stem: In the Point-of-Sale worked incorporation, what was the problem with the original system before the change?

options.text: It had no authorization — anyone was able to launch it
It stored business rules in the database
It had a fat IUser interface
It used inheritance as its primary reuse mechanism

questions.explanation: The deck states the old PoS had no authorization: 'anyone was able to launch it', motivating the cashier-login change request. B, C, and D are problems from the Clean Architecture and OO-principles decks, not the PoS incorporation example. (Actualization p.12)

questions.stem: What was the change request that drove the Point-of-Sale incorporation example?

options.text: Create a cashier login that controls user log in with a username and password
Widen the ZIP field from five to nine digits
Add a new RectangleFigure to the drawing editor
Switch the database from RDBMS to NoSQL

questions.explanation: The PoS change request was: 'Create a cashier login that will control the user log in with a username and password.' D is the database-detail example from Clean Architecture, C is a JHotDraw OCP example, and B is the small-change ZIP example. (Actualization p.12)

questions.stem: In the Point-of-Sale example, which new class is added to the model (Store, Inventory, Item, Price) and then incorporated?

options.text: FarmAnimal
Cashiers
PersistentStorage
Validator

questions.explanation: A new Cashiers class holding credentials is added to the model and incorporated. FarmAnimal is from the polymorphism example, PersistentStorage is the Pure Fabrication example, and Validator is the OCP loan example. (Actualization p.13)

questions.stem: Structurally, incorporating the new Cashiers class into the PoS launch path is an example of which incorporation shape?

options.text: Incorporating a new supplier (the launch/Store client now points at the new Cashiers supplier)
New responsibility local (a leaf nothing depends on)
Polymorphic extension of a composite responsibility
Replacement and deletion of an obsolete class

questions.explanation: The launch/Store client now depends on the new Cashiers class, so this is 'incorporating a new supplier.' It is not a local leaf (clients depend on it), not polymorphic extension (no subclass of an existing base is added), and not replacement (no class is removed). (Actualization p.11-14)

questions.stem: How does polymorphism work as an actualization mechanism, per the deck?

options.text: It extends a composite responsibility by adding a new subclass that implements an existing operation differently, without editing the clients
It splits a fat interface into client-specific interfaces
It deletes obsolete classes and reroutes all references
It widens a field inside one existing class

questions.explanation: Polymorphism lets you extend a composite responsibility by adding a new subclass that overrides an operation, leaving clients untouched. C is deletion/replacement, D is the small ZIP edit, B is ISP segregation. (Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: In the deck's polymorphism example, what holds the FarmAnimal objects, and which subclasses exist before Pig is added?

options.text: A Store holds Cashiers; subclasses Manager and Clerk exist
A Farm holds FarmAnimals; subclasses Cow and Sheep exist
An Address holds ZIPs; subclasses Short and Long exist
A Drawing holds Figures; subclasses Rectangle and Ellipse exist

questions.explanation: The example is a Farm holding FarmAnimals with subclasses Cow, Sheep (and Pig is added). A is the PoS example, D is the JHotDraw analogy, and C is a fabricated mix of the ZIP example. (Actualization p.6)

questions.stem: After adding the Pig subclass to the Farm example, what has happened structurally, in the deck's words?

options.text: The Farm now depends on a new external database supplier
The Farm class was rewritten to add a switch over animal types
The Cow and Sheep classes were deleted and replaced by Pig
The composite responsibility of Farm was extended by the concept Pig, and Farm's code is untouched

questions.explanation: The deck says 'the composite responsibility of Farm was extended by the concept Pig' — new behaviour is incorporated via inheritance and dynamic dispatch while Farm's code is untouched. B contradicts the whole point (no conditional added), C invents deletions, A invents a database dependency. (Actualization p.7)

questions.stem: What does adding the Pig subclass require, code-wise, in the Farm example?

options.text: A new constructor parameter on the Farm class
Editing every client that iterates over the Farm
Rerouting all references away from Cow and Sheep
Pure new code: a Pig class extending FarmAnimal that overrides makeSound()

questions.explanation: Adding Pig is pure new code — a Pig subclass of FarmAnimal overriding makeSound() (cout << 'Oink') — with no edits to clients. B and A contradict the untouched-client property; C is a deletion/replacement behaviour. (Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: Why is polymorphic extension described as the LEAST-rippling form of actualization?

options.text: Because subclasses are never compiled with the base class
Because the base class is deleted, removing all dependents
Because polymorphism disables change propagation entirely for the whole system
You add a leaf subclass and existing clients keep working through the base-class interface, so the change does not propagate to them

questions.explanation: Adding a leaf subclass keeps existing clients working through the base-class interface, so the change does not ripple to them — the least-rippling actualization. A is false (they compile together), B is false (nothing is deleted), C overstates it (propagation is bounded, not globally disabled). (Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: Polymorphic extension (add a Pig, don't edit Farm) is the direct embodiment of which OO principle and which GRASP pattern?

options.text: The Open/Closed Principle and GRASP Polymorphism
The Interface Segregation Principle and GRASP Indirection
The Liskov Substitution Principle and GRASP Controller
The Single Responsibility Principle and GRASP Creator

questions.explanation: Extending behaviour by adding a type rather than editing a conditional is exactly the Open/Closed Principle and GRASP Polymorphism. SRP/Creator, LSP/Controller, and ISP/Indirection are different pairings that do not describe 'extend by adding a subclass'. (Actualization p.6-7; OOPrinciples p.6, p.24)

questions.stem: A team adds a new animal type by inserting 'else if (type==PIG)' branches into Farm's existing methods instead of subclassing. Why does this contradict the deck's polymorphic-extension lesson?

options.text: It improves recall of the impact analysis automatically
It edits the clients (Farm's code) rather than adding a leaf subclass, so the change ripples and violates Open/Closed
It is a valid small change and matches the ZIP example exactly
It is required because C++ cannot dispatch dynamically

questions.explanation: The deck's point is that polymorphism extends behaviour by adding a subclass while leaving Farm untouched; editing Farm with type conditionals modifies the existing client, makes the change ripple, and violates Open/Closed. C mischaracterises a structural change as the trivial ZIP edit; D is false (D++ supports virtual dispatch, which the example uses); A is unrelated. (Actualization p.6-7; OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: What is change propagation?

options.text: The act of estimating which classes a change will impact before coding
After new code is incorporated, the process that seeks out and updates every place in the old code needing a secondary modification
The deletion of duplicated code prior to a change
The translation of a Response Model into a View Model

questions.explanation: Change propagation seeks out and updates every place in the old code requiring a secondary modification after incorporation. A is Impact Analysis, C is refactoring, D is the Presenter's job in Clean Architecture. (Actualization p.5; [ActLab])

questions.stem: What is the 'ripple effect' in the context of actualization?

options.text: A change to one class forcing changes in dependent classes, which in turn force further changes
An interface being segregated into smaller ones
The number of GRASP patterns growing from five to nine
A class becoming substitutable for its base class

questions.explanation: The ripple effect is a change spreading through dependencies: one class's change forces dependents to change, which forces further changes. D is LSP, C is a count confusion, B is ISP. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: In the deck's propagation walk, which classes form the animated dependency graph?

options.text: Entities, Use Cases, Interface Adapters, Frameworks
sale, register, saleLineItem, store, item, taxCategory
Singleton, Observer, MVC, MVVM, MVP
Cow, Sheep, Pig, Farm, FarmAnimal

questions.explanation: The propagation walk animates a dependency graph of sale, register, saleLineItem, store, item, and taxCategory. D is the polymorphism example, A is Clean Architecture layers, C is design patterns. (Actualization p.16-21)

questions.stem: During the propagation walk, what happens to a class's dependents after it is modified at the incorporation point?

options.text: They are moved into the innermost architecture layer
They are guaranteed to need no changes
They become inconsistent and must be visited and updated as the 'modified frontier' advances class by class
They are automatically deleted by the compiler

questions.explanation: Once a class is modified, its dependents become inconsistent and must be visited and updated; the modified frontier advances class by class. D and B are false, and A confuses propagation with layering. (Actualization p.16-20)

questions.stem: When does change propagation END, according to the deck?

options.text: When precision reaches 100%
When the impact set has been computed
When the database is switched to NoSQL
When no inconsistent classes remain — the system is internally consistent again

questions.explanation: Propagation ends when no inconsistent classes remain and the system is internally consistent again. A confuses termination with a precision score, B is Impact Analysis, C is an unrelated Clean Architecture example. (Actualization p.21)

questions.stem: The deck warns that a propagation that does NOT converge signals what?

options.text: Perfect recall
An over-segregated interface
A design problem
A successful small change

questions.explanation: Termination is the goal; a propagation that does not converge signals a design problem (excessive coupling). It is not a sign of good recall, a successful change, or interface segregation. (Actualization p.21)

questions.stem: Does deleting obsolete functionality cause change propagation?

options.text: Yes — all references to the deleted functionality must be deleted, and secondary changes propagate to other classes
Only if the deleted class was in the innermost layer
No — deletion is always free and never ripples
Only when precision is below 100%

questions.explanation: The deck states 'Deletion of obsolete functionality also causes change propagation. All references to the deleted functionality must be deleted; secondary changes propagate.' C is the trap (removing code is not free), and B/D invent irrelevant conditions. (Actualization p.22)

questions.stem: Why is change propagation called 'the moment of truth'?

options.text: Because it guarantees 100% recall
Because it confirms or refutes the predictions of Impact Analysis by actually modifying the impacted classes
Because it deletes the obsolete database
Because it is the first phase of the change process

questions.explanation: The deck says change propagation 'is the moment of truth: it confirms or refutes the predictions of impact analysis' by actually modifying the impacted classes. It is not the first phase, has nothing to do with the database, and does not guarantee recall (Ericsson recall was only 32%). (Actualization p.23)

questions.stem: Why does the deck say the accuracy of impact predictions is 'important for software managers'?

options.text: Because it drives effort estimates and therefore planning
Because it determines the number of GRASP patterns
Because it sets the database technology
Because managers write the new subclasses themselves

questions.explanation: Accurate predictions matter to managers because they drive effort estimates and planning. The other options are unrelated to why prediction accuracy concerns managers. (Actualization p.23)

questions.stem: A small, direct ZIP-style edit is made. A teammate claims 'small changes never propagate.' Why is that claim wrong?

options.text: Because all small changes are actually large changes in disguise
Because small changes skip the incorporation step and so always ripple more
Because the ZIP edit always switches the database
Even a direct edit can ripple, and deletion definitely propagates because all references to removed functionality must be updated

questions.explanation: The deck's gotcha is that even a direct edit can ripple, and deletion definitely propagates ('all references must be deleted'). A and C are false generalisations, and B inverts the relationship — skipping incorporation does not increase rippling. (Actualization p.22)

questions.stem: What is the formula for PRECISION of an impact analysis?

options.text: Precision = TN / (TN + FP)
Precision = TP / (TP + FN)
Precision = (TP + TN) / total
Precision = TP / (TP + FP)

questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP): of the classes you PREDICTED would change, what fraction actually did. B is the recall formula, A is specificity, C is accuracy. (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: What is the formula for RECALL of an impact analysis?

options.text: Recall = TP / (TP + FP)
Recall = TP / (TP + FN)
Recall = (TP + TN) / total
Recall = TN / (TN + FN)

questions.explanation: Recall = TP/(TP+FN): of the classes that ACTUALLY changed, what fraction did you predict. A is precision, D and C are unrelated metrics. (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson Radio Systems case study, what precision did the programmers achieve?
questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = 30/(30+0) = 100% — no false alarms. 32% was the RECALL; 68% is the fraction missed; 50% is a distractor. (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson case study, what recall did the programmers achieve?
questions.explanation: Recall = TP/(TP+FN) = 30/(30+64) = 0.32 = 32%. 100% was the PRECISION, 68% is the proportion missed, and 0% would mean no correct predictions. (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: Given the Ericsson matrix TP=30, FP=0, TN=42, FN=64, what is the precision?
questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = 30/(30+0) = 100%. B computes recall, A mislabels and uses TN, C uses the wrong denominator (TP+TN). (Actualization p.25-26)

questions.stem: Given the Ericsson matrix TP=30, FP=0, TN=42, FN=64, what is the recall?
questions.explanation: Recall = TP/(TP+FN) = 30/(30+64) = 0.32 = 32%. D is precision, B uses TN in the denominator by mistake, and A is a fabricated ratio. (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson confusion matrix, what is the value of true positives (TP)?

options.text: 0 (predicted changed but did not change)
64 (predicted unchanged but actually changed)
42 (predicted unchanged and actually unchanged)
30 (predicted changed and actually changed)

questions.explanation: TP = 30: classes predicted changed that actually changed. 64 = false negatives, 42 = true negatives, 0 = false positives. (Actualization p.25)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson confusion matrix, what is the value of false negatives (FN), and what do they represent?

options.text: 42 — classes predicted unchanged and which stayed unchanged
0 — classes predicted changed but which did not change
30 — classes predicted changed and which changed
64 — classes predicted unchanged but which actually changed (the dangerous ones)

questions.explanation: FN = 64: predicted unchanged but actually changed — the dangerous missed classes. 0 = false positives, 30 = true positives, 42 = true negatives. (Actualization p.25)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson confusion matrix, what is the value of false positives (FP)?
questions.explanation: FP = 0: no class was predicted to change but stayed unchanged — which is why precision is a perfect 100%. 30 = TP, 42 = TN, 64 = FN. (Actualization p.25)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson confusion matrix, what is the value of true negatives (TN)?
questions.explanation: TN = 42: classes predicted unchanged that stayed unchanged. 30 = TP, 64 = FN, 0 = FP. (Actualization p.25)

questions.stem: What is the TOTAL number of classes in the Ericsson study?
questions.explanation: Total = TN + FP + FN + TP = 42 + 0 + 64 + 30 = 136. 94 is the count of actually-changed classes (TP+FN), 72 is TP+TN, and 106 is a wrong sum. (Actualization p.24)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson matrix, how many classes ACTUALLY changed in total?

options.text: 136 (all classes)
30 (TP only)

questions.explanation: Actually-changed classes = TP + FN = 30 + 64 = 94 (the recall denominator). 30 counts only those correctly predicted, 42 is the unchanged-and-correct count, and 136 is the grand total. (Actualization p.24-27)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson matrix, how many classes were PREDICTED to change in total?

options.text: 94 (TP + FN)
136 (all classes)
64 (FN only)

questions.explanation: Predicted-changed = TP + FP = 30 + 0 = 30 (the precision denominator). 94 is the actually-changed count, 64 is FN, and 136 is the total. (Actualization p.24-26)

questions.stem: What does perfect (100%) precision mean in the Ericsson result?

options.text: Half the predictions were wrong
No misses — every class that changed was predicted
No false alarms — every class flagged really did need changing
The database was successfully swapped

questions.explanation: 100% precision (FP=0) means no false alarms: every flagged class really changed. 'No misses' describes recall (which was only 32%). The other options are unrelated. (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: The deck summarises the Ericsson recall result as the programmers estimating that the change would impact roughly what fraction of classes that actually changed?

options.text: All of them — they missed none
About two thirds — they missed the other third
About a third — they missed the other two thirds
None of them — they missed all

questions.explanation: The deck states programmers 'estimated that the changes will impact only about a third of all classes that actually changed — missed the other two thirds!' (32% recall). The other fractions contradict the 32% figure. (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: Which confusion-matrix category contains the DANGEROUS misses in impact analysis?

options.text: True positives (predicted changed and changed)
False positives (predicted changed but unchanged)
False negatives (predicted unchanged but actually changed)
True negatives (predicted unchanged and unchanged)

questions.explanation: False negatives are the dangerous ones — classes you thought were safe but that actually changed; they live in the recall denominator and blow schedules. False positives are merely false alarms; TP and TN are correct predictions. (Actualization p.25-27)

questions.stem: A student computes 'precision = 30/(30+64) = 32%' and calls it precision. What mistake was made?

options.text: They used the wrong numerator; precision should use TN
They forgot to add the true negatives to the numerator
They used the recall denominator (TP+FN); precision divides by predicted-changed (TP+FP), giving 30/30 = 100%
Nothing — 32% is the correct precision

questions.explanation: The student computed recall and mislabelled it. Precision = TP/(TP+FP) = 30/(30+0) = 100%; 30/(30+64) is recall = 32%. The numerator (TP) is correct; precision does not use TN. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes the DENOMINATORS of precision and recall?

options.text: Precision divides by actually-changed; recall divides by predicted-changed
Precision divides by predicted-changed (TP+FP); recall divides by actually-changed (TP+FN)
Both divide by the total number of classes
Precision divides by true negatives; recall divides by false positives

questions.explanation: The key gotcha is the denominator: precision's denominator is predicted-changed (TP+FP); recall's is actually-changed (TP+FN). A swaps them, C describes accuracy, and D names the wrong cells entirely. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson confusion matrix as drawn in the deck, which axis is which?

options.text: Rows = Predicted, columns = Actual
Rows = TP, columns = FN
Rows = Changed, columns = Precision
Rows = Actual, columns = Predicted

questions.explanation: In the deck's table, rows are Predicted and columns are Actual, so FN=64 sits in Predicted-Unchanged / Actual-Changed. D swaps the axes, and C/B mislabel them with metric/cell names. (Actualization p.24)

questions.stem: Why is impact routinely UNDER-estimated, according to the deck?

options.text: Because precision is always low
It is a consequence of invisibility — you cannot see all dependencies
Because programmers deliberately inflate estimates
Because the database is a detail

questions.explanation: Under-estimation is 'a consequence of invisibility' — hidden dependencies you cannot see — which makes planning difficult. C is the opposite behaviour, A is false (precision was 100%), and D is an unrelated Clean Architecture point. (Actualization p.28)

questions.stem: According to the deck, how widespread is under-estimation of impact?

options.text: A sign that recall was 100%
Caused by writing business rules in stored procedures
Common in software engineering and in other fields, and it makes planning difficult
Unique to the Ericsson study and never seen elsewhere

questions.explanation: The deck says under-estimation is 'common in software engineering, a consequence of invisibility', 'makes planning difficult', and is 'common in other fields also.' D contradicts that, A is false, and B is an unrelated Clean Architecture issue. (Actualization p.28)

questions.stem: The exam takeaway from the Ericsson study is that which metric is the HARD one to achieve, and what is the cost of doing it poorly?

options.text: Recall is hard; the cost of low recall is blown schedules (missed classes)
Accuracy is hard; the cost is too many true negatives
Specificity is hard; the cost is too many false alarms
Precision is hard; the cost of low precision is missed classes

questions.explanation: High precision is easy (Ericsson got 100%); high recall is hard (only 32%), and the cost of low recall is the missed classes that blow schedules. D inverts which metric is hard, and B/C name metrics not emphasised by the study. (Actualization p.27-28)

questions.stem: A manager wants to know which Ericsson metric to worry about for schedule risk. Why is recall (not precision) the answer?

options.text: Because precision below 100% always doubles the schedule
Because both metrics measure the same thing in this study
Because recall captures missed-but-changed classes (false negatives), the unplanned work that blows the schedule; precision only measures false alarms
Because precision counts unplanned work and recall counts false alarms

questions.explanation: Recall reflects false negatives — work you did not plan for — which is the schedule risk; precision only measures false alarms (over-prediction), which wastes some effort but does not surprise you. D swaps the meanings, B is false, and A is an invented rule. (Actualization p.27-28)

questions.stem: What does a 'software architecture' refer to, per the Clean Architecture deck?

options.text: A re-usable form of a solution to a local design problem
A class with a single reason to change
The high-level structures of a software system and the discipline of creating such structures
An interface implemented by an interactor

questions.explanation: The deck defines software architecture as 'the high-level structures of a software system and the discipline of creating such structures and systems.' A is the definition of a design pattern, B is SRP, and D is a boundary. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: What is a 'design pattern', per the Clean Architecture deck?

options.text: A concentric layer of business rules
A confusion-matrix category
The re-usable form of a solution to a design problem
The high-level structure of a whole system

questions.explanation: The deck defines a design pattern as 'the re-usable form of a solution to a design problem.' D is architecture, B is impact-analysis terminology, and A is a Clean Architecture layer. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: Which list contains only examples of ARCHITECTURES per the deck (not design patterns)?

options.text: Hexagonal, Onion, Clean
Singleton, Observer, MVC
MVVM, MVP, Repository
Creator, Controller, Indirection

questions.explanation: The deck's architecture examples are Hexagonal, Onion, and Clean. Singleton/Observer/MVC and MVVM/MVP are listed as design PATTERNS; Creator/Controller/Indirection are GRASP patterns. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: Which list contains only examples of DESIGN PATTERNS per the deck (not architectures)?

options.text: Singleton, Observer, MVC, MVVM, MVP
Hexagonal, Onion, Clean
Entities, Use Cases, Interface Adapters
Precision, Recall, Accuracy

questions.explanation: The deck lists Singleton, Observer, MVC, MVVM, and MVP as design patterns. Hexagonal/Onion/Clean are architectures, Entities/Use Cases/Interface Adapters are Clean Architecture layers, and Precision/Recall/Accuracy are metrics. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: What is the key distinction the deck draws between a design pattern and an architecture?

options.text: Patterns are always concentric circles; architectures are always linear
Patterns are inner layers; architectures are outer layers
Patterns organise the whole system; architecture solves local problems
Patterns solve local design problems; architecture organises the whole system

questions.explanation: Patterns solve local design problems, while architecture organises the whole system — they are different scopes. C inverts the scopes, and A/B invent shape/layer distinctions not in the deck. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: Who is credited with the Clean Architecture, per the deck?

options.text: Michael Feathers
Robert C. Martin
Craig Larman
Martin Fowler

questions.explanation: The Clean Architecture is credited to Robert A. Martin ('Clean Code'). Larman created GRASP, Feathers coined the SOLID acronym, and Fowler wrote the refactoring catalogue. (Clean Architecture p.5)

questions.stem: Which four characteristics define a successful (Clean) architecture?

options.text: Fast; small; written in Java; documented
High precision; high recall; low coupling; high cohesion
Singleton; Observer; MVC; MVVM
Testable; independent of the UI; independent of the database; independent of frameworks and external entities

questions.explanation: A successful architecture is testable and independent of the UI, the database, and frameworks/external entities. A lists unrelated qualities, C lists patterns, and B mixes metrics with GRASP qualities. (Clean Architecture p.3)

questions.stem: What does 'independent of the UI' mean as a characteristic of clean architecture?

options.text: The UI must be tested exhaustively
The UI can change easily without changing other system components
The UI is the innermost layer
The UI contains all the business rules

questions.explanation: Independent of the UI means 'UI can change easily, without changing other system components.' D contradicts the layering, A contradicts the 'View so stupid you don't test it' idea, and C is wrong — the UI is outermost. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: What does 'independent of the database' mean as a characteristic of clean architecture?

options.text: The database must use SQL only
The database holds all business logic in stored procedures
The database can be switched (e.g. RDBMS to NoSQL) without the business rules caring
The database is the center of the architecture

questions.explanation: Independent of the database means it can be switched from RDBMS to NoSQL or any other DB and the business rules do not care. B, D, and A all contradict the 'database is a detail' thesis. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: What does 'testable' mean as a characteristic of clean architecture?

options.text: Frameworks are tested before business rules
Every UI widget has an integration test
Business rules have tests that are independent of UI, database, and any external element
The database schema is validated on each commit

questions.explanation: Testable means business rules have tests independent of the UI, database, and external elements. The other options describe testing of outer/detail concerns, not the deck's testability characteristic. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: What does 'independent of frameworks' mean, per the deck (canonical Martin completion)?

options.text: Frameworks belong in the innermost Entities layer
Frameworks must be rewritten for each project
Frameworks define the business rules
Libraries and frameworks should be used as tools, rather than cramming your system into their constraints

questions.explanation: Independent of frameworks means 'libraries and frameworks should be used as tools, rather than having to cram your system into their constraints.' B, A, and C contradict the framework-as-detail principle. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: What does 'independent of any external agency' mean for the business rules?

options.text: Business rules should know nothing about the outside world
Business rules should live in the View
Business rules should call external web services directly
Business rules should depend on the database API

questions.explanation: The deck states 'business rules should know nothing about the outside world.' C, B, and D all couple business rules to outer/detail concerns, the opposite of the principle. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: How does each 'independence' characteristic relate to future change requests?

options.text: Each independence increases coupling to the database
Each independence is a hedge against a category of future change — e.g. a 'change the UI' request ripples only through the outer layer
Each independence is a design pattern, not an architectural concern
Each independence guarantees the code can never change again

questions.explanation: Each independence hedges a category of change: if the UI is independent, a 'change the UI' request ripples only through the outer layer; if the DB is independent, a 'swap DB' request never reaches the entities. D overstates it, A inverts it, and C miscategorises it. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: Why does keeping the architecture testable, UI-independent, DB-independent and framework-independent make ACTUALIZATION cheaper?

options.text: Because it forces every change to be a small ZIP-style edit
Because it deletes obsolete classes automatically
Because it removes the need for Impact Analysis entirely
Because each independence bounds how far a corresponding change request can ripple, keeping change propagation short

questions.explanation: The independences bound the ripple of each change category, so propagation stays short and actualization is cheap — the architectural reason for low maintenance cost. C is false (Impact Analysis is still needed), A is false, and B is unrelated. (Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: How is the Clean Architecture drawn?

options.text: As concentric circles, from innermost (most stable) to outermost (most volatile)
As a single flat list of classes
As an inheritance tree of FarmAnimals
As a confusion matrix

questions.explanation: Clean Architecture is drawn as concentric circles from inner (most stable) to outer (most volatile). B, D, and C are unrelated shapes from other parts of the course. (Clean Architecture p.5-8)

questions.stem: What is the INNERMOST layer of the Clean Architecture?

options.text: Use Cases
Entities
Frameworks and Drivers
Interface Adapters

questions.explanation: Entities are the innermost layer (enterprise-wide business rules, least likely to change). Frameworks and Drivers is the outermost; Interface Adapters and Use Cases sit between. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: What is the OUTERMOST layer of the Clean Architecture?

options.text: Use Cases
Frameworks and Drivers
Interface Adapters
Entities

questions.explanation: Frameworks and Drivers is the outermost layer (web framework, database, UI, HTTP client). Entities is innermost, with Use Cases and Interface Adapters between. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: What is the correct order of the four Clean Architecture layers from INNER to OUTER?

options.text: Use Cases -> Entities -> Frameworks & Drivers -> Interface Adapters
Entities -> Use Cases -> Interface Adapters -> Frameworks & Drivers
Frameworks & Drivers -> Interface Adapters -> Use Cases -> Entities
Interface Adapters -> Entities -> Use Cases -> Frameworks & Drivers

questions.explanation: Inner to outer: Entities, Use Cases, Interface Adapters, Frameworks & Drivers. C is the outer-to-inner order, and A/D scramble the layers. (Clean Architecture p.5-7)

questions.stem: What do the Entities (innermost layer) contain?

options.text: The web framework and database drivers
Enterprise-wide business rules — the most general rules, least likely to change
Presenters, ViewModels, and Gateways
Application-specific business rules called interactors

questions.explanation: Entities encapsulate enterprise-wide business rules, the most general and least likely to change. A is Frameworks & Drivers, D is Use Cases, and C is Interface Adapters. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: What do the Use Cases layer (also called interactors) contain?

options.text: The View that grabs data from the View Model
The web, devices, and external interfaces
Application-specific business rules, isolated from changes to the database, frameworks, and UI
Enterprise-wide, application-independent business rules

questions.explanation: Use Cases (interactors) contain application-specific business rules and are isolated from DB, framework, and UI changes. D describes Entities, B describes Frameworks & Drivers, and A describes the View. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: Which components does the Interface Adapters layer include, per the deck?

options.text: Request Models and Response Models only
Entities and enterprise business rules
The web framework, database, and HTTP client
Presenters (from MVP), ViewModel (from MVVM), and Gateways (a.k.a. Repositories)

questions.explanation: Interface Adapters include Presenters (MVP), ViewModel (MVVM), and Gateways/Repositories, plus Controllers. B is the Entities layer, C is Frameworks & Drivers, and A names only the data structures, not the adapters. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: What does the Interface Adapters layer DO?

options.text: Displays strings and flags on the screen
Converts data from a format convenient for entities and use cases to a format applicable to databases and the web
Controls the dance of the entities
Holds the least-likely-to-change enterprise business rules

questions.explanation: Interface Adapters convert data between the inner format (for entities/use cases) and the outer format (DB/web). D is Entities, C is the interactor, and A is the View. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: Which items belong to the Frameworks & Drivers (outermost) layer?

options.text: Request and Response Models
Entities and enterprise business rules
Presenters, ViewModels, Gateways
Web framework, database, UI, HTTP client, devices, external interfaces

questions.explanation: Frameworks & Drivers is the outermost layer: web framework, database, UI, HTTP client, devices, external interfaces. C is Interface Adapters, B is Entities, and A are data structures crossing boundaries. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: What does the Dependency Rule state?

options.text: Dependencies may point in any direction as long as they compile
Dependencies must point toward the database
Source-code dependencies point only inward (toward stable abstractions)
Source-code dependencies point only outward (toward volatile details)

questions.explanation: The Dependency Rule: source-code dependencies point only inward, toward stable abstractions (Entities). D is the exact reversal (the classic gotcha), A ignores the rule, and B points at a detail. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: Under the Dependency Rule, which layers know about which?

options.text: No layer knows about any other layer
Every layer knows about every other layer
Inner layers know about outer layers; outer layers know nothing about inner layers
Outer layers know about inner layers; inner layers know nothing about outer layers

questions.explanation: Outer layers know about inner layers; inner layers must know nothing about outer layers. C inverts it, and B/A ignore the rule. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: The deck's pyramid axis runs from 'Abstract, General, Rarely Change' at the top to what at the bottom?

options.text: Concrete, General, Never Change
Concrete, Specific, Change Frequently
Tested, Independent, Stable
Abstract, Specific, Rarely Change

questions.explanation: The pyramid axis runs from 'Abstract, General, Rarely Change' (Entities, top) to 'Concrete, Specific, Change Frequently' (External Interfaces, bottom). The other options garble that gradient. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: In the deck's flow diagram, what is at the centre, with the Dependency Rule arrows pointing toward it?

options.text: The UI (Entity -> Use case -> Presenter -> UI)
The Entity (UI -> Presenter -> Use case -> Entity)
The Presenter
The database

questions.explanation: The flow shows UI -> Presenter -> Use case -> Entity at the centre, with the Dependency Rule arrows pointing toward the Entity. A reverses it, and D/C place a detail or adapter at the stable centre. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: Why must source-code dependencies point INWARD in the Clean Architecture?

options.text: Because the database must be at the center
Inner layers are stable abstractions and outer layers are volatile details, so pointing inward makes details depend on abstractions, never the reverse
Because inner layers change most often and need protection
Because the UI is the most stable component

questions.explanation: Inner = stable abstractions, outer = volatile details; pointing inward means details depend on abstractions (the Dependency Inversion Principle at architecture scale). C inverts stability, and A/D contradict the layering. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: The Dependency Rule (dependencies point inward toward stable abstractions) is the architectural-scale form of which OO principle?

options.text: The Composite Reuse Principle
The Interface Segregation Principle
The Dependency Inversion Principle — details depend on abstractions
The Single Responsibility Principle

questions.explanation: Depending inward so details depend on abstractions IS the Dependency Inversion Principle applied at architecture scale. SRP (one reason to change), ISP (client-specific interfaces), and CRP (composition over inheritance) address different concerns. (Clean Architecture p.8; OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: A developer makes the Entities layer import a class from the Frameworks & Drivers layer. Why does this violate Clean Architecture?

options.text: It points a source-code dependency outward, breaking the Dependency Rule that inner layers must know nothing about outer layers
It points a dependency inward, which is forbidden
It is allowed because Entities are the most volatile layer
It is fine as long as it compiles

questions.explanation: Entities (inner) importing from Frameworks & Drivers (outer) points a dependency outward, violating the Dependency Rule — inner layers must know nothing about outer layers. B mislabels the direction, C inverts volatility, and D ignores the rule. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: In the Clean Architecture data-flow vocabulary, what does the Interactor contain?

options.text: Application-independent business rules
The strings and flags ready for display
The web framework and HTTP client
Application-specific business rules

questions.explanation: The Interactor (Use Case) contains application-specific business rules and 'controls the dance of the entities.' A describes the Entity, B describes the View Model, and C describes Frameworks & Drivers. (Clean Architecture p.9)

questions.stem: In the data-flow vocabulary, what does the Entity contain?

options.text: Application-independent business rules
The delivery mechanism's UI code
Application-specific business rules
Primitive-typed request data

questions.explanation: The Entity contains application-INDEPENDENT business rules. C is the Interactor's content, B is the delivery mechanism, and D is the Request Model. (Clean Architecture p.9)

questions.stem: What is a Boundary in the Clean Architecture data flow?

options.text: A View that displays strings
A concrete database connection class
The innermost entity object
An interface/protocol through which data enters and leaves the interactor

questions.explanation: A Boundary is an interface/protocol: data comes into interactors through a boundary implemented by the interactor, and goes out to boundaries implemented by other objects. B, C, and A are concrete/other components, not the boundary interface. (Clean Architecture p.9)

questions.stem: The interactor receives the request model because of what relationship with the input boundary?

options.text: The interactor implements the input boundary
The interactor is a subclass of the View
The interactor inherits from the request model
The interactor stores the request model in the database

questions.explanation: Because the interactor implements the input boundary, passing the request model through that boundary delivers it to the interactor. C, B, and D describe relationships that do not exist in the data flow. (Clean Architecture p.10)

questions.stem: What can the Delivery Mechanism be, per the deck?

options.text: Only a relational database
Only a Presenter
Web, console application, mobile app UI, etc.
Only the Entities layer

questions.explanation: The deck says the delivery mechanism 'can be Web, Console application, Mobile App UI, etc.' The other options wrongly restrict it to a DB, an inner layer, or a single adapter. (Clean Architecture p.9)

questions.stem: What is the Request Model, and what kind of data does it hold?

options.text: An entity object with full business behaviour
A database connection pool
A presentable structure of formatted strings and flags
A plain data structure of primitive types only, packing the submitted data for the interactor

questions.explanation: The delivery mechanism packs submitted data into the Request Model — a plain data structure of primitive types only. C describes the View Model, A an entity, and B a framework detail. (Clean Architecture p.10)

questions.stem: After the interactor processes a request, where does it gather the results?

options.text: Into the Request Model
Into a stored procedure
Into a Response Model
Directly into the View

questions.explanation: The interactor gathers results into a Response Model, passed back out through the output boundary. A is the input structure, D skips the Presenter/View Model, and B violates the 'no business rules in the DB' rule. (Clean Architecture p.12)

questions.stem: The deck says the interactor uses the request model and does what with the entities?

options.text: Deletes all entities and recreates them
Translates the entities into a view model
Controls the dance of the entities (creates the order, modifies the customer, etc.)
Stores the entities directly in the View

questions.explanation: The interactor 'controls the dance of the entities' — creating the order, modifying the customer, etc. A is destructive nonsense, D bypasses layers, and B is the Presenter's job. (Clean Architecture p.11)

questions.stem: What is the Response Model, per the deck's definition?

options.text: A relational table in the database
The View itself
A structure of display-ready strings and boolean flags
Data created by the interactor as the result of the use case; not presentable (a date is a date object, currency is a money object)

questions.explanation: The Response Model is the interactor's result data, NOT presentable — a date is a date object, currency a money object. C describes the View Model (presentable), A a DB table, and B the View. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: What is the job of the Presenter?

options.text: Hold the application-independent business rules
Take the Response Model and translate it into a View Model (e.g. a nice date string, currency symbol)
Pack primitive request data for the interactor
Talk directly to the Database API

questions.explanation: The Presenter takes the Response Model and translates it into a View Model — generating a nice date string, adding the currency symbol, etc. (it is from MVP). A is an Entity, C is the delivery mechanism, and D is the Gateway. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: What does the View Model contain, per the deck?

options.text: The application-specific business rules
Primitive request data from the form
Strings and flags ready for display — e.g. the button's label string and a boolean for whether the button is active
Date objects and money objects not yet formatted

questions.explanation: The View Model contains display-ready strings and flags: the button-label string, a boolean for active/inactive, menu-item names and order. D is the Response Model (not yet presentable), A is the interactor, and B is the Request Model. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: What is the job of the View, per the deck?

options.text: Grab the data from the View Model and put it on the screen — 'so stupid you don't have to test it'
Implement the input boundary
Translate the Response Model into a View Model
Control the dance of the entities

questions.explanation: The View just grabs data from the View Model and puts it on screen — 'so stupid you don't have to test it.' C is the Presenter, D is the interactor, and B is the interactor again. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: What is the correct OUTBOUND order of the assembled data flow (Figure 1.6) after the interactor produces results?

options.text: Request Model -> Boundary -> Interactor -> Entities
Presenter -> Interactor -> Entities -> Response Model
Response Model -> Boundary -> Presenter -> View Model -> View
View -> View Model -> Presenter -> Boundary -> Response Model

questions.explanation: Outbound: Response Model -> Boundary -> Presenter -> View Model -> View. D reverses it, A is the INBOUND flow, and B scrambles the components. (Clean Architecture p.14)

questions.stem: What is the correct INBOUND order of the assembled data flow before the interactor runs?

options.text: Entities -> Interactor -> Boundary -> Controller
Controller -> Boundary -> Interactor -> Entities
View -> View Model -> Presenter -> Boundary
Presenter -> View Model -> View

questions.explanation: Inbound: Controller -> Boundary -> Interactor -> Entities. C and D describe the outbound presentation path, and A reverses the inbound order. (Clean Architecture p.14)

questions.stem: Why must the View be 'so stupid you don't have to test it', with all formatting done by the Presenter?

options.text: Because the View must talk directly to the database
So that all presentation logic lives in the testable Presenter/View Model and the untestable View holds no logic worth testing
Because the View contains the application-specific business rules
Because the View implements the input boundary

questions.explanation: Putting formatting in the Presenter (which produces the View Model) keeps testable logic out of the View, so the View is trivial. C, D, and A assign the View responsibilities it must not have. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: What is the deck's thesis about the database?

options.text: The database is the innermost stable layer
The database is a detail; it shouldn't be the center of your architecture
The database should hold all the business rules
The database is a design pattern

questions.explanation: The deck states 'the database is a detail, it shouldn't be the center of your architecture' and should be a plug-in to the business rules. C, A, and D all contradict the database-as-detail thesis. (Clean Architecture p.17)

questions.stem: Where should business rules NOT be written, per the deck?

options.text: In the use-case layer
In the entities
In stored procedures in the database
In the interactor

questions.explanation: The deck says 'business rules shouldn't be written in stored procedures in the database.' Business rules belong in the entities and use cases (interactor), not the database. (Clean Architecture p.17)

questions.stem: Martin's rule of thumb on the slide says: if something changes a lot it should be X, and if it doesn't change often it should be Y. What are X and Y?

options.text: X = a plug-in; Y = plugged into
X = plugged into; Y = a plug-in
X = a design pattern; Y = an architecture
X = an entity; Y = a framework

questions.explanation: Martin: 'If something changes a lot, it should be a plug-in. If something doesn't change very often, it should be plugged into.' So volatile things plug into stable things, never the reverse. B inverts it; D and C miscategorise. (Clean Architecture p.16)

questions.stem: In the Entity Gateway pattern, where is the Entity Gateway INTERFACE declared, and where is its IMPLEMENTATION?

options.text: Both the interface and implementation sit in the database
Both are declared in the View
The interface is in the detail layer; the implementation is in the entities
The interface is declared with the business rules; the implementation sits below the boundary line in the detail layer

questions.explanation: The Entity Gateway interface is declared WITH the business rules, but implemented OUT in the detail layer (the Entity Gateway Implementation talks to the Database API). This keeps the dependency pointing inward. A, B, and C misplace the parts. (Clean Architecture p.18)

questions.stem: The Entity Gateway is the same as which pattern named earlier in the deck?

options.text: Presenter
Observer
Repository
Singleton

questions.explanation: The Entity Gateway is the Repository pattern named on the Interface Adapters slide. Singleton and Observer are unrelated design patterns, and Presenter is the MVP component on the output side. (Clean Architecture p.7, p.18)

questions.stem: Why does the Entity Gateway keep the dependency pointing inward even though it reaches the database?

options.text: Because the gateway moves business rules into the database

Because the interactor depends on the abstract gateway interface (declared with business rules); the concrete implementation in the detail layer depends inward on that interface — Dependency Inversion
Because the gateway deletes the entities
Because the interactor depends directly on the concrete Database API

questions.explanation: The interactor depends on the abstract gateway interface; the concrete implementation depends inward on that interface — Dependency Inversion keeps the arrow inward despite touching the DB. D couples to a concretion, and A/C are nonsense. (Clean Architecture p.18)

questions.stem: Because the DB is a plug-in behind a gateway interface, a 'switch from SQL to NoSQL' change request is actualized entirely where?

options.text: In the View, because it shows the data
Across all four layers equally
In the entities, because they own the data
In the Entity Gateway Implementation — propagation cannot reach the entities or use cases

questions.explanation: With the DB behind a gateway interface, the SQL->NoSQL change is actualized only in the Entity Gateway Implementation; propagation cannot reach entities or use cases. The architecture bounds the ripple. C, A, and B spread the change where the gateway prevents it from reaching. (Clean Architecture p.16-18)

questions.stem: What does the Entity Gateway Implementation talk to, below the boundary line?

options.text: The View Model
The Database API
The Presenter
The Request Model

questions.explanation: The Entity Gateway Implementation sits below the boundary line and talks to the Database API. The View Model, Request Model, and Presenter are on the presentation/data-flow side, not the persistence side. (Clean Architecture p.18)

questions.stem: What does the SOLID acronym stand for?

options.text: Substitutability, Openness, Low coupling, Indirection, Decoupling
Singleton, Observer, Liskov, Indirection, Delegation
Stable, Object-oriented, Layered, Independent, Decoupled
Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion

questions.explanation: SOLID = Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion. The other options invent expansions mixing in unrelated patterns/principles. (OOPrinciples p.3)

questions.stem: Who introduced the theory of the SOLID principles, and in which paper?

options.text: Craig Larman, in 'Applying UML and Patterns'
Martin Fowler, in 'Refactoring'
Michael Feathers, in 'Working Effectively with Legacy Code'
Robert C. Martin, in his 2000 paper 'Design Principles and Design Patterns'

questions.explanation: The SOLID theory was introduced by Robert C. Martin in his 2000 paper 'Design Principles and Design Patterns.' Larman is GRASP, Feathers coined the SOLID acronym (but didn't introduce the theory), and Fowler wrote Refactoring. (OOPrinciples p.2)

questions.stem: Who introduced the SOLID ACRONYM itself (as distinct from the principles)?

options.text: Robert C. Martin
Craig Larman
Michael Feathers
Erich Gamma

questions.explanation: The SOLID acronym was introduced later by Michael Feathers; Robert B. Martin introduced the underlying principles. Larman created GRASP and Gamma co-authored the Gang of Four design-patterns book. (OOPrinciples p.2)

questions.stem: The deck opens the SOLID history with which striking statistic?

options.text: 80% of software projects fail
100% of databases use SQL
9 out of 5 projects succeed
32% of impact predictions are correct

questions.explanation: The deck opens with '80% of software projects fail' as motivation for SOLID. 32% is the Ericsson recall, the SQL claim is unrelated, and '9 out of 5' is nonsensical. (OOPrinciples p.2)

questions.stem: How many principles are in SOLID, and how many GRASP patterns are there?

options.text: 9 SOLID principles and 5 GRASP patterns
5 SOLID principles and 9 GRASP patterns
9 of each
5 of each

questions.explanation: There are exactly 5 SOLID principles and 9 GRASP patterns. The deck's gotcha is not to merge the two counts: SOLID is five, GRASP is nine. (OOPrinciples p.3, p.18)

questions.stem: Which one-line definition matches the 'S' in SOLID?

options.text: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
A class should have only a single responsibility
Depend upon abstractions, not concretions
Objects should be replaceable with instances of their subtypes

questions.explanation: The 'S' (Single Responsibility) one-liner is 'a class should have only a single responsibility.' A is OCP, D is LSP, and C is DIP. (OOPrinciples p.3)

questions.stem: Which one-line definition matches the 'I' in SOLID?

options.text: Open for extension, closed for modification
Subclasses should be substitutable for their base classes
A class should have only a single responsibility
Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface

questions.explanation: The 'I' (Interface Segregation) one-liner is 'many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface.' C is SRP, B is LSP, and A is OCP. (OOPrinciples p.3)

questions.stem: Why is SOLID described as an 'umbrella' of OO design principles?

options.text: Because it groups five complementary principles that together guide maintainable OO design
Because it is a single principle with five names
Because it replaces all nine GRASP patterns
Because it is a design pattern from the Gang of Four

questions.explanation: SOLID is an umbrella over five complementary principles guiding maintainable OO design. It is not one principle (B), it does not replace GRASP (C), and it is not a GoF design pattern (D). (OOPrinciples p.2-3)

questions.stem: What is the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP), in Martin's words?

options.text: A class should be substitutable for its base class
A class should depend on abstractions, not concretions
A class should have only one reason to change
A class should be open for extension but closed for modification

questions.explanation: Martin's SRP states a class should have only one reason to change (equivalently, a single responsibility). D is OCP, B is DIP, and A is LSP. (OOPrinciples p.4)

questions.stem: In the SRP deck example, which class violates SRP by both changing passwords AND verifying access?

options.text: LoanApprovalHandler
SecurityService
UserService (it both changePassword and checkAccess)
Payments

questions.explanation: The 'without S' UserService both changes passwords and verifies access (checkAccess) — two reasons to change. SecurityService is the EXTRACTED class in the fixed version; LoanApprovalHandler is the OCP example, and Payments is the DIP example. (OOPrinciples p.5)

questions.stem: How is the SRP violation fixed in the deck example?

options.text: Access checking is split into its own SecurityService class
A polymorphic subclass is added
An interface is segregated into IUser and IRole
The two responsibilities are merged into one larger method

questions.explanation: The fix splits access checking into a separate SecurityService, so UserService calls SecurityService.checkAccess(). D is the opposite of SRP, C is the ISP fix, and B is the OCP/polymorphism technique. (OOPrinciples p.5)

questions.stem: After applying SRP in the deck example, why is the design better?

options.text: Password policy and access policy each have one reason to change, so changes to one don't affect the other
The View no longer needs testing
The database is now a plug-in
Both policies now live in a single class for convenience

questions.explanation: After the split, password policy and access policy each have one reason to change, isolating future changes. D contradicts SRP, and C/B are unrelated Clean Architecture points. (OOPrinciples p.5)

questions.stem: How does SRP help limit change propagation during actualization?

options.text: If each class has many responsibilities, fewer classes need touching
SRP eliminates the need for incorporation
If each class has one responsibility, a change request touches few classes, so impact-analysis recall improves and ripple is short
SRP guarantees 100% recall on every change

questions.explanation: With one responsibility per class, a change touches few classes, improving recall and shortening ripple. A inverts the logic, D overstates SRP, and B is false (incorporation is still needed). (OOPrinciples p.4-5)

questions.stem: A class handles both 'how to format an invoice' and 'how to compute tax'. Which principle does this violate, and why?

options.text: DIP, because the class depends on an abstraction
LSP, because the class isn't substitutable for its base
ISP, because the class implements too many interfaces
SRP, because the class has two reasons to change (formatting rules and tax rules)

questions.explanation: Two unrelated reasons to change (formatting and tax) violate SRP. It is not LSP (no substitution issue), not ISP (that concerns interfaces, not class responsibilities), and DIP (depending on an abstraction) is actually good, not a violation. (OOPrinciples p.4)

questions.stem: What is the Open/Closed Principle (OCP)?

options.text: A class should have only one reason to change
Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
Many specific interfaces are better than one general interface
Subclasses should be substitutable for their base classes

questions.explanation: OCP: entities should be 'open for extension, but closed for modification.' A is SRP, D is LSP, and C is ISP. (OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: What does OCP let us add WITHOUT modifying pre-existing classes?

options.text: New false negatives
New databases
New bugs
New features

questions.explanation: OCP states 'we should be able to add new features to our system without having to modify our set of pre-existing classes.' The other options are not what OCP enables. (OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: One tenet of OCP is to reduce coupling to what level?

options.text: The database level — relate every class to the DB
The concrete level — relate two concrete classes directly
The abstract level — relate a concrete class to an abstract class or interface, not to another concrete class
The UI level — relate every class to the View

questions.explanation: OCP reduces coupling to the abstract level: 'instead of creating relationships between two concrete classes, we create relationships between a concrete class and an abstract class or an interface.' B is the opposite, and A/D are unrelated. (OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: In the OCP deck example, what is wrong WITHOUT 'O'?

options.text: LoanApprovalHandler.approve is hard-wired to a concrete PersonalLoanValidator, so a new loan type means editing the handler
Payments news a concrete CreditCardPaymentMethod
UserService both changes passwords and checks access
Ostrich extends Bird but throws on fly()

questions.explanation: Without OCP, LoanApprovalHandler is hard-wired to the concrete PersonalLoanValidator, so supporting a new loan type requires editing the handler. C is the SRP example, D is LSP, and B is DIP. (OOPrinciples p.7)

questions.stem: How is the OCP example fixed?

options.text: Introduce a Validator interface; PersonalLoanValidator and HomeLoanValidator implement it, and LoanApprovalHandler depends on Validator
Delete HomeLoanValidator and keep only PersonalLoanValidator
Store the validation rules in the database
Merge all loan logic into LoanApprovalHandler

questions.explanation: The fix introduces a Validator interface implemented by PersonalLoanValidator and HomeLoanValidator; LoanApprovalHandler depends on the abstraction and is closed to modification but open to new validators. D, B, and C all defeat the extensibility OCP provides. (OOPrinciples p.7)

questions.stem: After applying OCP, how is LoanApprovalHandler best described?

options.text: A subclass of Validator
Closed to modification but open to new validators
Dependent on the concrete PersonalLoanValidator
Open to modification but closed to new validators

questions.explanation: After the fix, LoanApprovalHandler is closed to modification yet open to new validators (you add a new Validator implementation, not edit the handler). D inverts it, C is the pre-fix state, and A is wrong (the handler depends on, not subclasses, Validator). (OOPrinciples p.7)

questions.stem: OCP is the design embodiment of which actualization technique from the Actualization deck?

options.text: Polymorphic extension — add a Pig subclass, don't edit Farm
Widening a ZIP field inside one class
Computing precision and recall
Deleting an obsolete class

questions.explanation: OCP embodies the polymorphic-extension technique: add a new type (a Pig), don't modify the existing client (Farm). B is the small-change ZIP edit, D is deletion, and C is impact scoring. (OOPrinciples p.6-7; Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)?

options.text: Open for extension, closed for modification
Depend upon abstractions, not concretions
Subclasses should be substitutable for their base classes
A class should have only one reason to change

questions.explanation: LSP (the substitutability principle): subclasses should be substitutable for their base classes without altering program correctness. D is SRP, A is OCP, and B is DIP. (OOPrinciples p.8)

questions.stem: LSP is also known by which other name?

options.text: The Law of Demeter
The single-responsibility principle
The Composite Reuse Principle
The substitutability principle

questions.explanation: LSP is also called the substitutability principle. The Law of Demeter is PLK, and CRP is composition-over-inheritance — different principles. (OOPrinciples p.8)

questions.stem: In the LSP deck example, why does Ostrich extends Bird violate LSP?

options.text: Ostrich cannot eat()
Ostrich's fly() throws UnsupportedOperationException, so an Ostrich is not substitutable for a Bird where fly() is called
Ostrich depends on a concrete class
Ostrich has too many responsibilities

questions.explanation: Ostrich extends Bird but overrides fly() to throw, breaking any client that calls fly() — it is not substitutable. A is false (it can eat), D is SRP, and C is DIP. (OOPrinciples p.9)

questions.stem: How is the LSP example fixed?

options.text: Move fly() into the database
Delete the Bird base class
Restructure the hierarchy so only flight-capable birds expose fly() (e.g. FlightBird vs NonFlightBird)
Make Ostrich's fly() return null instead of throwing

questions.explanation: The fix restructures the hierarchy: Bird has eat(), FlightBird adds fly(), NonFlightBird does not; Sparrow extends FlightBird, Ostrich extends NonFlightBird. D still breaks the contract, and B/A are nonsense. (OOPrinciples p.9)

questions.stem: Why is the LSP gotcha that 'Ostrich extends Bird compiles but is still wrong'?

options.text: LSP is only about whether the code compiles, which it does, so it is fine
LSP is about the behavioural contract, not just compiling — Ostrich compiles but throws on fly(), breaking substitutability
LSP requires Ostrich to override eat() instead
LSP forbids inheritance entirely
