questions.explanation: The gotcha: LSP concerns the behavioural contract, not compilation. Ostrich compiles but throwing on fly() violates substitutability. A misses the point, C is irrelevant, and D overstates it. (OOPrinciples p.9)

questions.stem: How does LSP protect polymorphic actualization?

options.text: LSP guarantees the new subclass never compiles
LSP forces all subclasses into the database layer
LSP makes the impact set empty
If a newly added subclass violates the base contract, incorporating it introduces defects and causes propagation rather than absorbing it

questions.explanation: LSP protects polymorphic extension: a contract-violating subclass introduces defects on incorporation, causing rather than absorbing propagation. A, B, and C misstate LSP's effect. (OOPrinciples p.8-9; Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: A Square subclass of Rectangle overrides setWidth to also change height, breaking clients that set width and height independently. Which principle is violated and why?

options.text: SRP, because Square has two reasons to change
OCP, because Rectangle was modified
ISP, because Rectangle's interface is too fat
LSP, because Square is not behaviourally substitutable for Rectangle even though it compiles

questions.explanation: Square violates LSP: it compiles as a Rectangle but breaks the behavioural contract clients rely on (independent width/height), so it is not substitutable. This is the same shape as the Ostrich/Bird example. It is not SRP, ISP, or OCP. (OOPrinciples p.8-9)

questions.stem: What is the Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)?

options.text: Subclasses must be substitutable for base classes
Many specific interfaces are better than a single, general interface
Favor composition over inheritance
A class should have only one reason to change

questions.explanation: ISP: 'many specific interfaces are better than a single, general interface' and any interface should be highly cohesive. D is SRP, A is LSP, and C is CRP. (OOPrinciples p.10)

questions.stem: What property should any interface have under ISP?

options.text: It should be highly cohesive
It should contain every method any client might need
It should depend on a concrete class
It should be highly coupled

questions.explanation: ISP says any interface we define should be highly cohesive. D, C, and B describe the opposite of good interface design. (OOPrinciples p.10)

questions.stem: In the ISP deck example, what is the problem WITHOUT 'I'?

options.text: One fat interface IUser(changePassword, checkUserRole, assignRole) forces every implementer to carry methods it doesn't need
Ostrich throws on fly()
UserService has two reasons to change
Payments news a concrete payment method

questions.explanation: Without ISP, one fat IUser interface bundles changePassword, checkUserRole and assignRole, forcing implementers to carry unneeded methods. B is LSP, D is DIP, and C is SRP. (OOPrinciples p.11)

questions.stem: How is the ISP example fixed?

options.text: Replace the interface with a concrete class
Merge all methods into one IEverything interface
Move the methods into the database
Split into cohesive, client-specific interfaces: IUser(changePassword), IRole(assignRole), IUserRole(checkUserRole)

questions.explanation: The fix splits the fat interface into IUser, IRole, and IUserRole, so clients depend only on the slice they use. B is the opposite of ISP, and A/C abandon interfaces entirely. (OOPrinciples p.11)

questions.stem: How does ISP confine change propagation, compared with a fat interface?

options.text: Fat interfaces broaden coupling so a change to one method's signature ripples to all implementers; segregated interfaces confine that ripple
ISP eliminates all ripple permanently
Fat interfaces confine ripple; segregated interfaces broaden it
Both fat and segregated interfaces ripple identically

questions.explanation: A fat interface couples all implementers, so a signature change ripples to all of them; segregating interfaces confines the ripple to the relevant clients. C inverts it, D ignores the difference, and B overstates it. (OOPrinciples p.10-11)

questions.stem: What is the difference in SCOPE between SRP and ISP?

options.text: They are the same principle with different names
SRP is about interfaces; ISP is about classes
SRP is about databases; ISP is about UIs
SRP is about a class having one reason to change; ISP is about interfaces being client-specific and cohesive

questions.explanation: SRP concerns a CLASS having one reason to change; ISP concerns INTERFACES being client-specific and cohesive — they rhyme but differ in scope. B swaps them, A conflates them, and C is unrelated. (OOPrinciples p.4, p.10)

questions.stem: A printer interface forces a simple printer to implement print(), scan(), and fax() even though it can't scan or fax. Which principle is violated?

options.text: DIP, because the printer depends on a concretion
LSP, because the printer isn't substitutable
ISP, because the fat interface forces an implementer to carry methods it doesn't need
SRP, because the printer class has three reasons to change

questions.explanation: Forcing a simple printer to implement scan() and fax() it can't support is the classic ISP violation — split into focused interfaces. It is not SRP (the issue is the interface, not class responsibilities), not LSP, and not DIP. (OOPrinciples p.10-11)

questions.stem: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)?

options.text: Many specific interfaces beat one general interface
A class should have only one reason to change
Subclasses must be substitutable for base classes
Depend upon abstractions; do not depend upon concretions

questions.explanation: DIP: 'Depend upon abstractions. Do not depend upon concretions.' A is ISP, B is SRP, and C is LSP. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: DIP states we should couple at which level?

options.text: The abstract level, not the concrete level
The UI level
The database level
The concrete level, not the abstract level

questions.explanation: DIP formalizes abstract coupling: 'we should couple at the abstract level, not at the concrete level.' D is the opposite, and C/B are unrelated. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: What key relationship between DIP and OCP does the deck state?

options.text: DIP replaces OCP entirely
DIP and OCP are mutually exclusive
OCP tells us how we can adhere to DIP
DIP tells us how we can adhere to OCP

questions.explanation: The deck explicitly says 'DIP tells us how we can adhere to OCP' — depending on abstractions is the mechanism that keeps entities closed to modification but open to extension. C inverts it, and B/A are false. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: In the DIP deck example, what is wrong WITHOUT 'D'?

options.text: IUser bundles too many methods
Payments news a concrete CreditCardPaymentMethod, welding it to credit-card logic
UserService both changes passwords and checks access
Ostrich throws on fly()

questions.explanation: Without DIP, Payments instantiates a concrete CreditCardPaymentMethod (ccpm = new ...), so it is welded to credit-card logic. C is SRP, D is LSP, and A is ISP. (OOPrinciples p.13)

questions.stem: How is the DIP example fixed?

options.text: Payments is deleted and replaced
Payments stores the payment method in the database
Payments depends on the abstract PaymentMethod, injected via the constructor (constructor dependency injection)
Payments creates a new concrete method inside makePayments()

questions.explanation: The fix injects an abstract PaymentMethod via the constructor (Payments(PaymentMethod pm_provided)), so Payments no longer knows the concrete method. D is the pre-fix behaviour, and A/B are unrelated. (OOPrinciples p.13)

questions.stem: The DIP fix uses which concrete technique to supply the abstraction?

options.text: Constructor dependency injection
Inheritance from PaymentMethod
A Singleton factory in the database
A static global variable

questions.explanation: The deck achieves DIP with plain constructor dependency injection — passing the PaymentMethod into the constructor. It is not a Singleton, not inheritance, and not a global. (OOPrinciples p.13)

questions.stem: What is the DIP gotcha regarding 'DI frameworks'?

options.text: DIP only works if the database is NoSQL
DIP requires a specific dependency-injection framework to work
DIP is the principle (depend on abstractions); the deck achieves it with plain constructor injection — no framework needed
DIP and dependency injection are completely unrelated

questions.explanation: The gotcha: Dependency Inversion is the principle, achieved here by plain constructor injection — no DI framework required. B is false, D is wrong (DI is the technique that realises DIP here), and A is irrelevant. (OOPrinciples p.13)

questions.stem: How is DIP the same idea as the Clean Architecture Dependency Rule, and how does the Entity Gateway relate?

options.text: Both make details depend on abstractions with dependencies pointing toward the stable abstraction; the Entity Gateway is DIP made architectural
DIP is a GRASP pattern, not related to architecture
The Entity Gateway violates DIP by depending on the database
DIP points dependencies outward; the Dependency Rule points them inward, so they conflict

questions.explanation: DIP at class scale and the Dependency Rule at architecture scale both make details depend on abstractions, with dependencies pointing toward the stable abstraction; the Entity Gateway is DIP made architectural. D claims a false conflict, C is wrong, and B miscategorises DIP. (OOPrinciples p.12; Clean Architecture p.8, p.18)

questions.stem: What does the Composite Reuse Principle (CRP) say?

options.text: Depend on concretions, not abstractions
Favor inheritance over composition as the primary reuse mechanism
Give every class many reasons to change
Favor polymorphic composition of objects over inheritance

questions.explanation: CRP: 'Favor polymorphic composition of objects over inheritance.' B is the catastrophic mistake CRP warns against, A inverts DIP, and C inverts SRP. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: Per the CRP slide, what is described as a 'catastrophic mistake' in OO systems?

options.text: Using composition as the primary reuse mechanism
Injecting dependencies through constructors
Using inheritance as the primary reuse mechanism
Using interfaces to decouple classes

questions.explanation: The slide calls 'using inheritance as the primary reuse mechanism' one of the most catastrophic mistakes. Composition (A), interfaces (D), and constructor injection (B) are all good practices the deck endorses. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: What does the CRP slide propose as a better alternative to inheritance?

options.text: Global variables
Fat interfaces
Stored procedures
Delegation

questions.explanation: The slide says 'Delegation can be a better alternative to inheritance.' Stored procedures, global variables, and fat interfaces are all discouraged elsewhere in the decks. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: Why does composition/delegation localise change better than deep inheritance for maintenance?

options.text: Both spread change identically
Composition removes the need for change propagation
Inheritance is run-time and flexible; composition is compile-time and rigid
Composition is run-time and flexible (swap a delegate); deep inheritance is compile-time and spreads change up and down the tree

questions.explanation: Composition/delegation is run-time and flexible (swap a delegate), localising change; deep inheritance is compile-time and rigid, spreading change through the tree. C inverts the properties, A ignores the difference, and B overstates it. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: A teammate cites CRP to argue 'we should never use inheritance.' Why is that an overstatement of the slide?

options.text: The slide says delegation is catastrophic
The slide says composition should never be used
The slide says inheritance is always forbidden in OO design
The slide favors composition and calls inheritance-as-primary-reuse catastrophic, but inheritance is still correct for genuine is-a polymorphism (the Farm/Pig and OCP examples use it)

questions.explanation: CRP favors composition and condemns inheritance-as-PRIMARY-reuse, but inheritance is still correct for genuine is-a polymorphism — the Farm/Pig and OCP examples deliberately use it. So 'never inherit' overstates the slide. C, B, and A misread it. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: The Principle of Least Knowledge is also called what?

options.text: The Liskov Substitution Principle
The Single Responsibility Principle
The Law of Demeter
The Composite Reuse Principle

questions.explanation: The Principle of Least Knowledge is also called the Law of Demeter. LSP, CRP, and SRP are distinct principles. (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: Under the Principle of Least Knowledge, for an operation O on a class C, which objects' operations may be called?

options.text: Only objects stored in the database
Only objects in the View Model
Any object reachable through a chain of getters
Itself, its parameters, objects it creates, and its contained instance objects

questions.explanation: PLK allows calling operations on: itself, its parameters, objects it creates, and its contained instance fields. C is exactly what PLK forbids (transitive visibility), and A/B are unrelated restrictions. (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: What does the Law of Demeter tell you to AVOID?

options.text: Calling methods on your parameters
Calling methods on objects you create
Calling methods on your own instance fields
Calling methods on an object obtained by calling a method on another object (transitive visibility)

questions.explanation: PLK avoids 'calling any methods on an object where the reference to that object is obtained by calling a method on another object' (transitive visibility). C, A, and B are exactly the calls PLK PERMITS. (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: What is the benefit of following the Law of Demeter, per the deck?

options.text: The database becomes the center of the architecture
The interface becomes fatter and more general
The calling method doesn't need to understand the structural makeup of the object it invokes methods upon
The calling method can reach any object in the system

questions.explanation: The benefit is that 'the calling method doesn't need to understand the structural makeup of the object it's invoking methods upon.' D is the opposite, and A/B are unrelated. (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: Which call is a 'train wreck' that the Law of Demeter warns against?

options.text: a.getB().getC().doThing()
createdObject.doThing()
this.doThing()
parameter.doThing()

questions.explanation: a.getB().getC().doThing() is a train wreck reaching through transitive getters — exactly what PLK forbids. Calling on this, a parameter, or a created object are all permitted. (OOPrinciples p.15)

questions.stem: How does PLK relate to the Ericsson impact under-estimation problem?

options.text: PLK guarantees 100% recall on every change
PLK increases transitive coupling so more classes change
PLK minimises transitive coupling, which is the invisible coupling that makes change propagation explode and recall hard to achieve
PLK only applies to the database layer

questions.explanation: PLK minimises transitive coupling — precisely the invisible dependencies behind Ericsson's low recall and exploding propagation. B inverts it, A overstates it, and D wrongly limits its scope. (OOPrinciples p.15; Actualization p.27-28)

questions.stem: What does GRASP stand for?

options.text: Global Responsibility Architecture Support Protocol
Grouped Re-usable Architecture Software Principles
Generic Refactoring And Software Patterns
General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns

questions.explanation: GRASP = General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns. The other expansions are invented. (OOPrinciples p.16)

questions.stem: How many GRASP patterns are there?

options.text: Nine
Three
Five
Seven

questions.explanation: There are exactly nine GRASP patterns (vs five SOLID principles). Don't merge the counts. (OOPrinciples p.18)

questions.stem: Who introduced the GRASP patterns, and in which book?

options.text: Martin Fowler, in 'Refactoring'
Robert C. Martin, in 'Design Principles and Design Patterns'
Craig Larman, in 'Applying UML and Patterns'
The Gang of Four, in 'Design Patterns'

questions.explanation: GRASP was introduced by Craig Larman in 'Applying UML and Patterns' (3rd Ed., 2004). Martin authored SOLID, the Gang of Four authored the design-patterns catalogue, and Fowler wrote Refactoring. (OOPrinciples p.16-17)

questions.stem: What is the primary concern of the GRASP patterns?

options.text: Responsibility assignment in object-oriented design
Confusion-matrix scoring
Database schema design
Version-control branching

questions.explanation: GRASP describes fundamental principles for OO design and RESPONSIBILITY ASSIGNMENT, expressed as patterns. The other options are unrelated concerns. (OOPrinciples p.16)

questions.stem: When does the deck say the assignment of responsibilities often occurs?

options.text: Only during the Conclusion phase
During the creation of interaction diagrams, and certainly during programming
Only when computing precision and recall
Only when switching databases

questions.explanation: The deck says assignment of responsibilities 'often occurs during the creation of interaction diagrams, and certainly during programming.' The other options are unrelated phases/activities. (OOPrinciples p.16)

questions.stem: Which list contains exactly the nine GRASP patterns?

options.text: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion, Creator, Controller, Indirection, Polymorphism
Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller, Polymorphism, Indirection, Pure Fabrication, Composite Reuse
Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller, Polymorphism, Indirection, Pure Fabrication, Protected Variations
Singleton, Observer, Factory, Adapter, Facade, Strategy, Mediator, Proxy, Decorator

questions.explanation: The nine GRASP patterns are Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller, Polymorphism, Indirection, Pure Fabrication, Protected Variations. A mixes in SOLID, D lists GoF patterns, and B wrongly substitutes Composite Reuse (a SOLID-supporting principle) for Protected Variations. (OOPrinciples p.18)

questions.stem: Which statement correctly separates the SOLID and GRASP lists?

options.text: SOLID has nine and GRASP has five
SOLID and GRASP are the same nine items under two names
SOLID has five principles (SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP); GRASP has nine responsibility-assignment patterns — the lists must not be merged
GRASP is a subset of SOLID

questions.explanation: SOLID = five principles; GRASP = nine patterns; the gotcha is not to merge the counts or the lists. B, A, and D all conflate or miscount the two sets. (OOPrinciples p.3, p.18)

questions.stem: What does the GRASP Information Expert pattern say?

options.text: Assign a responsibility to the class with the fewest fields
Assign a responsibility to the class that has the information necessary to fulfill it
Assign a responsibility to the UI class
Assign a responsibility to a made-up convenience class

questions.explanation: Information Expert: assign a responsibility to the class that has the information to fulfill it — put behaviour where the data lives. A is arbitrary, D is Pure Fabrication, and C contradicts the Controller pattern. (OOPrinciples p.19)

questions.stem: Under GRASP Creator, a container class should create instances of a part class under which condition?

options.text: The container aggregates, contains, records, closely uses the part, or has the part's initializing data
The container is in a different package from the part
The container has the fewest responsibilities
The container is the UI controller

questions.explanation: Creator: a container creates a part when it aggregates, contains, records, closely uses the part, or has the part's initializing data. The other options are not Creator conditions. (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: Under GRASP Creator, which relationship is the deck's PREFERRED reason for one class to create another?

options.text: The creating class aggregates or contains the created objects
The creating class is a subclass of the other class
The two classes are unrelated
The creating class is itself created by the other class

questions.explanation: Creator prefers the class that aggregates or contains the created objects. 'Created by the other class' reverses the relationship, and the remaining options are not Creator conditions. (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: What does GRASP Low Coupling advise?

options.text: Assign responsibilities so that coupling is maximised
Assign all responsibilities to one god class
Assign responsibilities so that coupling remains low
Assign responsibilities to the database

questions.explanation: Low Coupling: assign responsibilities so coupling remains low. High coupling makes classes fragile, hard to understand in isolation, and hard to reuse. The other options describe poor design. (OOPrinciples p.21)

questions.stem: Per the Low Coupling pattern, why is a highly coupled class undesirable?

options.text: It has no dependencies at all
It is too cohesive
It has only one reason to change
Changes in related classes force local changes; it is harder to understand in isolation and harder to reuse

questions.explanation: A highly coupled class is undesirable because changes in related classes force local changes, and it is harder to understand in isolation and harder to reuse (it requires its dependencies present). C and A describe GOOD properties, and B is the wrong direction. (OOPrinciples p.21)

questions.stem: What does GRASP High Cohesion advise?

options.text: Put all responsibilities in the View
Make each class do many unrelated things
Assign responsibilities so that cohesion remains high
Assign responsibilities so that cohesion is minimised

questions.explanation: High Cohesion: assign responsibilities so cohesion remains high. Low cohesion (a class doing many unrelated things) is undesirable. The other options describe low cohesion or misplaced responsibilities. (OOPrinciples p.22)

questions.stem: Per the High Cohesion pattern, how is a class with LOW cohesion described?

options.text: Having exactly one reason to change
Easy to comprehend and never affected by change
Hard to comprehend, hard to reuse, hard to maintain, and delicate (constantly affected by change)
The most stable class in the system

questions.explanation: Low-cohesion classes do too much and are 'hard to comprehend, hard to reuse, hard to maintain, and delicate (constantly affected by change).' The other options describe desirable or unrelated properties. (OOPrinciples p.22)

questions.stem: Who should own a 'calculate the total' responsibility under GRASP Information Expert?

options.text: A made-up PersistentStorage class
Whichever class has the fewest methods
The class that holds the line items and prices needed to compute the total
The window or view class

questions.explanation: Information Expert assigns the responsibility to the class holding the necessary information (the line items/prices). A made-up class is Pure Fabrication, the view must delegate (Controller), and method count is irrelevant. (OOPrinciples p.19)

questions.stem: What does the GRASP Controller pattern assign?

options.text: The responsibility for receiving or handling a system event message to a façade or use-case/session controller
The responsibility for computing a total to the data's owner
The responsibility for creating instances to whichever class aggregates them
The responsibility for storing objects to a PersistentStorage class

questions.explanation: Controller assigns the handling of a system event to a non-UI class: either a façade controller (system/device/subsystem) or a use-case/session controller. D is Pure Fabrication, C is Creator, and B is Information Expert. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: Which classes does the GRASP Controller pattern explicitly say should NOT handle system events?

options.text: Façade controllers and use-case controllers
Window, applet, widget, view, and document classes — they should delegate to a controller
Information experts and creators
Entity and use-case classes

questions.explanation: The Controller pattern explicitly excludes window, applet, widget, view, and document classes — these UI classes should delegate system events to a controller, not handle them. A names the classes that SHOULD handle events, and D/C are unrelated. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: An exam question asks whether a Swing JFrame (window) should directly process a 'submit order' system event. What does GRASP say?

options.text: Yes — the window is a valid use-case controller
No — but only because the window has low cohesion
Yes — the window is the natural Information Expert for system events
No — window/view classes must delegate system events to a controller; they should not fulfill system-event tasks themselves

questions.explanation: GRASP Controller's note excludes window/view classes from handling system events; they must delegate to a controller. So the JFrame should NOT process it directly. C and A wrongly cast the window as expert/controller, and B gives the wrong reason. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: What does the GRASP Polymorphism pattern advise when behaviors vary by type?

options.text: Use a long if/else or switch on the type code
Store the behavior in the database
Put the behavior in the window class
Assign responsibility using polymorphic operations to the types for which the behavior varies, defined in a base class or preferably an interface

questions.explanation: GRASP Polymorphism: when behaviors vary by type, use polymorphic operations on the varying types, defined in a base class or (preferably) an interface — replace type conditionals with polymorphism. A is exactly what it replaces, and B/C misplace the behavior. (OOPrinciples p.24)

questions.stem: GRASP Polymorphism is the same idea as which earlier example/principle?

options.text: The SRP UserService/SecurityService split
The confusion-matrix precision calculation
The actualization Pig/Farm example and the Open/Closed Principle — replace conditionals on type with polymorphism
The Law of Demeter train-wreck rule

questions.explanation: GRASP Polymorphism mirrors the Pig/Farm actualization example and OCP — extend by adding a type, replacing conditionals. A is SRP, D is PLK, and B is impact scoring. (OOPrinciples p.24; Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: What does the GRASP Indirection pattern assign?

options.text: The responsibility to a made-up persistence class
The responsibility to an intermediate object that mediates between components so they are not directly coupled
The responsibility to handle system events to a controller
The responsibility to the class with the most information

questions.explanation: Indirection introduces an intermediate object to mediate between components so they aren't directly coupled. D is Information Expert, C is Controller, and A is Pure Fabrication. (OOPrinciples p.25)

questions.stem: The GRASP Indirection pattern warns to beware of what?

options.text: Constructor injection
High cohesion
Transitive visibility
Low coupling

questions.explanation: Indirection says 'beware of transitive visibility' — the same concern as the Law of Demeter. High cohesion and low coupling are desirable, and constructor injection is a good DIP technique. (OOPrinciples p.25)

questions.stem: What does the GRASP Pure Fabrication pattern assign responsibilities to?

options.text: A window or view class
An artificial or convenience class that does not represent a problem-domain concept
The class that aggregates the created objects
The class that already has the necessary domain information

questions.explanation: Pure Fabrication assigns a cohesive set of responsibilities to a made-up class that is NOT a problem-domain concept, to gain cohesion, low coupling, and reuse. D is Information Expert, A is excluded by Controller, and C is Creator. (OOPrinciples p.26)

questions.stem: Which example does the deck give for GRASP Pure Fabrication?

options.text: A Validator interface for loan approval
A Cashiers class for the Point-of-Sale system
A PersistentStorage class solely responsible for saving objects to a relational database
A FlightBird subclass of Bird

questions.explanation: The Pure Fabrication example is a PersistentStorage class — a 'figment of the imagination' responsible only for saving objects to a relational DB. The Validator is the OCP example, Cashiers is the incorporation example, and FlightBird is the LSP fix. (OOPrinciples p.26)

questions.stem: Pure Fabrication's PersistentStorage example corresponds to which Clean Architecture idea?

options.text: The Request Model — packing primitive input
The Gateway/Repository — keeping persistence out of domain entities
The Dependency Rule arrow pointing outward
The View Model — holding display strings

questions.explanation: A made-up PersistentStorage class keeps persistence out of domain entities — exactly the Clean Architecture Gateway/Repository idea. The View Model and Request Model are data-flow structures, and the Dependency Rule points inward, not outward. (OOPrinciples p.26; Clean Architecture p.7, p.18)

questions.stem: What does the GRASP Protected Variations pattern advise?

options.text: Identify points of predicted variation or instability and assign responsibilities to create a stable interface around them
Maximise coupling between volatile components
Put all variation in the window class
Store variation points in stored procedures

questions.explanation: Protected Variations: identify predicted points of variation/instability and wrap them behind a stable interface (interface in the broad sense of an access view). B, C, and D are anti-patterns. (OOPrinciples p.27)

questions.stem: In Protected Variations, the word 'interface' is used in what sense?

options.text: The broadest sense of an access view — not literally only a Java or COM interface
Only a graphical user interface
Only a database connection
Only a literal Java interface keyword

questions.explanation: The deck notes 'interface' here means the broadest sense of an access view, not literally only a Java or COM interface. D, B, and C wrongly narrow the term. (OOPrinciples p.27)

questions.stem: Why is Protected Variations called the 'grand unifying' GRASP pattern, with OCP, DIP and Indirection as special cases?

options.text: Because it is the only pattern that ignores variation points
Because all of them wrap a predicted point of change behind a stable seam, which is exactly what Protected Variations generalises
Because it only applies to the database
Because it replaces the other eight GRASP patterns entirely

questions.explanation: Protected Variations generalises 'wrap what will change behind a stable interface' — OCP, DIP, and Indirection are all special cases of that seam-building idea. A is the opposite, D overstates it, and C wrongly limits its scope. (OOPrinciples p.27)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern answers 'who should be responsible for CREATING object X'?

options.text: Creator
Protected Variations
Controller
Information Expert

questions.explanation: 'Who creates X?' is answered by Creator (the class that aggregates/contains/records/closely-uses X or has its init data). Information Expert answers 'who has the data', Controller handles system events, and Protected Variations wraps unstable points. (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern answers 'who should HANDLE a system event'?

options.text: Creator
Pure Fabrication
Controller
Low Coupling

questions.explanation: 'Who handles a system event?' is answered by Controller (a façade or use-case controller, never a UI/view class). Creator handles object creation, Pure Fabrication handles made-up convenience classes, and Low Coupling is a quality goal. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern answers 'behavior varies by type — how do I assign it'?

options.text: Indirection
Polymorphism
Creator
High Cohesion

questions.explanation: 'Behavior varies by type?' is answered by GRASP Polymorphism (use polymorphic operations on the varying types). Indirection decouples via an intermediary, High Cohesion is a quality goal, and Creator handles object creation. (OOPrinciples p.24)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern answers 'I need to wrap an UNSTABLE point that I expect to change'?

options.text: High Cohesion
Information Expert
Creator
Protected Variations

questions.explanation: 'Wrap an unstable point?' is answered by Protected Variations (create a stable interface around the predicted variation). Information Expert places behaviour with data, Creator handles creation, and High Cohesion is a quality goal. (OOPrinciples p.27)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern answers 'I need a made-up class to hold persistence logic so my domain entities stay clean'?

options.text: Information Expert
Low Coupling
Pure Fabrication
Controller

questions.explanation: A made-up class (e.g. PersistentStorage) for persistence is GRASP Pure Fabrication. Information Expert would put it with the data-owner (polluting the entity), Controller handles events, and Low Coupling is a quality goal. (OOPrinciples p.26)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern answers 'I need an intermediary so two components are not directly coupled'?

options.text: High Cohesion
Polymorphism
Creator
Indirection

questions.explanation: An intermediary to avoid direct coupling is GRASP Indirection. Polymorphism handles type-varying behavior, Creator handles creation, and High Cohesion is a quality goal. (OOPrinciples p.25)

questions.stem: A class relies on many other classes, so changes elsewhere force local changes and it is hard to reuse. Which GRASP goal is being violated?

options.text: Low Coupling
Pure Fabrication
Creator
High Cohesion

questions.explanation: Relying on many classes (so external changes force local changes, and reuse is hard) is a Low Coupling violation. High Cohesion is about a class doing too many unrelated things, and Pure Fabrication/Creator are unrelated. (OOPrinciples p.21)

questions.stem: A class does many unrelated things and too much work, making it hard to comprehend and delicate. Which GRASP goal is being violated?

options.text: Low Coupling
Indirection
Controller
High Cohesion

questions.explanation: Doing many unrelated things (hard to comprehend, delicate) violates High Cohesion. Low Coupling concerns dependencies on other classes, and Controller/Indirection are different patterns. (OOPrinciples p.22)

questions.stem: Assigning save-to-database behaviour to a domain Entity would satisfy Information Expert but harm the design. Which GRASP pattern resolves the tension, and how?

options.text: Pure Fabrication — invent a non-domain PersistentStorage class so the entity keeps high cohesion and low coupling
Creator — let the entity create its own database connection
Controller — make the window save the entity
Low Coupling — add more dependencies to the entity

questions.explanation: Putting persistence on the entity (Information Expert) hurts cohesion/coupling; Pure Fabrication resolves it by inventing a PersistentStorage class. C violates Controller's UI exclusion, B couples the entity to the DB, and D worsens coupling. (OOPrinciples p.22, p.26)

questions.stem: A use case must work whether figures are stored in SQL or NoSQL, a predicted variation. Which GRASP pattern and which SOLID principle BOTH apply?

options.text: Creator and the Liskov Substitution Principle
Controller and the Interface Segregation Principle
Protected Variations and the Dependency Inversion Principle — wrap the store behind a stable interface and depend on the abstraction
High Cohesion and the Single Responsibility Principle

questions.explanation: Wrapping the predicted storage variation behind a stable interface is Protected Variations, realised via DIP (depend on the abstract store). The other pairings address cohesion, creation, or event handling, not the storage-variation seam. (OOPrinciples p.12, p.27)

questions.stem: What is JHotDraw, the course's standard case study?

options.text: A Java GUI framework for structured drawing editors
A confusion-matrix calculator
A relational database engine
A version-control system

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is a Java GUI framework for structured drawing editors, used to ground every change-process phase. The other options are unrelated tools. (JHotDraw Connection; [ActLab])

questions.stem: What does the Actualization lab require you to provide regarding SOLID and Clean Architecture?

options.text: Examples of the SOLID principles and Clean Architecture in the context of the CASE study
A migration of JHotDraw to NoSQL
A rewrite of JHotDraw in another language
A proof that JHotDraw has no technical debt

questions.explanation: The lab requires examples of the SOLID principles and Clean Architecture IN CONTEXT OF the CASE study. The other options are not lab deliverables. [ActLab]

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, which hierarchy is the textbook OCP/GRASP-Polymorphism case?

options.text: The Figure hierarchy (RectangleFigure, EllipseFigure, LineFigure extending/implementing Figure)
The confusion-matrix categories
The Cashiers/Store/Inventory model
The Bird/Ostrich hierarchy

questions.explanation: JHotDraw's Figure hierarchy (RectangleFigure, EllipseFigure, LineFigure on a Figure abstraction) is the textbook OCP/GRASP-Polymorphism case: a new figure type is new code plugged in with clients untouched. The other options are from different examples. (JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: Adding a new Figure type to JHotDraw leaves DrawingView/DrawingEditor clients untouched. This is the JHotDraw form of which actualization shape?

options.text: Replacement and deletion of an obsolete class
Incorporating a new supplier that all clients must adjust to
Widening a field in one class (small change)
Polymorphic extension — 'add a Pig, don't edit Farm' — new code plugged in via the Figure abstraction

questions.explanation: Adding a Figure subtype leaves clients untouched through the Figure interface — the polymorphic-extension shape ('add a Pig, don't edit Farm'). It is not replacement/deletion, not a one-field small change, and not a new supplier that forces client edits. (JHotDraw Connection; Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw mapped onto Clean Architecture, which classes play the Entities/Use-Cases role?

options.text: DrawingView and the Swing components
The HTTP client and database drivers
The drawing model (Drawing, Figure, Handle) — application-independent and application-specific business rules
The Presenter and View Model

questions.explanation: The drawing model (Drawing, Figure, Handle) plays the Entities/Use-Cases role, while DrawingView, Swing components, and tools play the Interface Adapters / Frameworks & Drivers role. A and B are the outer layers, and D are data-flow adapters. (JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: Under the Dependency Rule, a 'swap the GUI toolkit' change request in JHotDraw should ripple only through which layers?

options.text: The innermost Entities layer only
The outer layers (Interface Adapters / Frameworks & Drivers), not the model entities
The database gateway only
All four layers equally

questions.explanation: Because the model is insulated by the Dependency Rule, a 'swap the GUI toolkit' request ripples only through the outer layers, not the model entities — the same argument as the database being a plug-in. A, D, and C misplace the ripple. (JHotDraw Connection; Clean Architecture p.16-18)

questions.stem: When the exam asks for a JHotDraw example of OCP/LSP/Polymorphism, which structure should you choose?

options.text: The Figure polymorphism hierarchy
The 80%-of-projects-fail statistic
The ZIP-widening edit
The confusion matrix

questions.explanation: For OCP/LSP/Polymorphism, choose the Figure polymorphism hierarchy. The confusion matrix is impact scoring, the ZIP edit is a small change, and the statistic is SOLID motivation. (JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: For a JHotDraw example of ISP, which structure does the guide suggest?

options.text: The confusion-matrix denominators
A god class handling all events
The single fat Figure base class
An interface-segregated handle/tool API

questions.explanation: For ISP, the guide suggests an interface-segregated handle/tool API (client-specific interfaces). A fat base class and a god class violate ISP, and the confusion matrix is unrelated. (JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: For a JHotDraw example of DIP/Creator, which structure does the guide suggest?

options.text: Dependency-injected / Factory-created figures
Figures that new their own concrete dependencies
The Bird/Ostrich hierarchy
The 32% recall figure

questions.explanation: For DIP/Creator, choose dependency-injected or Factory-created figures. Figures newing their own concretions violate DIP, Bird/Ostrich is the LSP example, and 32% is the Ericsson recall. (JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: When actualizing a JHotDraw change like 'add a new tool', how do you score your Impact-Analysis prediction afterward?

options.text: Build a confusion matrix of predicted vs actually-touched Figure/Tool/View classes and compute precision and recall
Count the number of GRASP patterns used
Check whether the View needed testing
Measure how many stored procedures changed

questions.explanation: After propagation, you score the prediction by building a confusion matrix of predicted vs actually-touched classes and computing precision/recall — the Ericsson method applied to the case study. The other options are not the scoring method. (JHotDraw Connection; Actualization p.24-27)

questions.stem: A change request 'add a new tool' to JHotDraw is incorporated and then you walk which dependency chain to propagate?

options.text: Precision -> Recall -> Accuracy
Cow -> Sheep -> Pig
Entities -> Database -> Stored procedures
Tool -> DrawingEditor -> DrawingView, updating each dependent until consistency is restored

questions.explanation: You walk Tool -> DrawingEditor -> DrawingView (the JHotDraw dependency graph), propagating until consistency is restored. The other options are not dependency chains in JHotDraw. (JHotDraw Connection; Actualization p.16-21)

questions.stem: In the PoS cashier-login walkthrough, why is this a LARGER change rather than a small ZIP-style edit?

options.text: It deletes an obsolete class
It widens a single field in one existing class
It adds a brand-new responsibility (authentication), so a new class is written separately then incorporated
It only renames an existing method

questions.explanation: Adding authentication is a brand-new responsibility, so it is a larger change: write a new Cashiers class separately, then incorporate it. B is the small-change technique, and D/A are not what the change does. (Actualization p.5, p.12)

questions.stem: When implementing the new Cashiers class in isolation, applying SRP means what?

options.text: Cashiers only authenticates; it does not also price items
Cashiers handles authentication, pricing, and inventory together
Cashiers is stored as a stored procedure
Cashiers is a subclass of Bird

questions.explanation: SRP applied to Cashiers means it ONLY authenticates and does not also price items — one reason to change. B bundles unrelated responsibilities (anti-SRP), and D/C are unrelated. (Actualization p.12; OOPrinciples p.4-5)

questions.stem: When implementing Cashiers, applying DIP means the credential store is reached how?

options.text: By writing the login logic in a stored procedure
Through an abstract, injected credential store — in Clean-Architecture terms, an Entity Gateway/Repository interface
By having the View query the database directly
By newing a concrete database connection inside Cashiers

questions.explanation: DIP means depending on an abstract, injected credential store — an Entity Gateway/Repository interface in Clean-Architecture terms. D couples to a concretion, A puts logic in the DB, and C bypasses the layers. (Actualization p.12; OOPrinciples p.12-13; Clean Architecture p.18)

questions.stem: In the PoS walkthrough, after Cashiers is incorporated, which code becomes inconsistent and must be updated during propagation?

options.text: The launch code and any client that assumed 'anyone can launch' must now branch on auth state
The confusion matrix
Only the Cashiers class itself
Nothing — incorporation never causes propagation

questions.explanation: Incorporation makes the launch code and any 'anyone can launch' client inconsistent; they must branch on auth state, and the UI must show a login screen. C, B, and D deny the propagation that incorporation triggers. (Actualization p.12-14, p.16-21)

questions.stem: In a clean architecture, where should the new login SCREEN changes be confined during the PoS walkthrough?

options.text: The Request Model primitives
The Entity Gateway interface declaration
The innermost Entities layer
The outer Frameworks & Drivers / View layer only

questions.explanation: If the architecture is clean, the login-screen changes are confined to the outer Frameworks & Drivers / View layer. The entities, the gateway interface, and the request model are insulated from UI-look changes. (Actualization p.12-14; Clean Architecture p.4)

questions.stem: In the PoS walkthrough, why does a clean architecture keep the SQL-vs-NoSQL credential-store choice and the login-screen look as mere 'details'?

options.text: Because details always reach the entities
Because the login use case (interactor) is insulated from the UI and DB, so those choices plug in and propagation is bounded to the gateway implementation and the view
Because the interactor stores its rules in the database
Because the View is the most stable component

questions.explanation: The login interactor is insulated from UI and DB, so the store choice and screen look are plug-in details; propagation is bounded to the gateway implementation and the view, never the business entities. C, D, and A contradict the insulation. (Worked Example; Clean Architecture p.4, p.16-18)

questions.stem: After propagation in the PoS walkthrough, what do you do to verify the Impact-Analysis prediction?

options.text: Switch the database to NoSQL
Compare the classes actually touched against the prediction, build a confusion matrix, and compute precision and recall
Delete the Cashiers class and start over
Count the SOLID principles used

questions.explanation: Verification compares actually-touched vs predicted classes via a confusion matrix and computes precision/recall — low recall is the 'moment of truth' showing under-estimation. The other options are not the verification step. (Worked Example; Actualization p.24-27)

questions.stem: In the PoS walkthrough, after the feature works, which phase cleans up the code, and under what continuous activity?

options.text: Prefactoring, then Initiation, under Concept Location
Postfactoring, then Conclusion, all under continuous Verification
Replacement, then deletion, under Indirection
Impact Analysis, then Actualization, under no verification

questions.explanation: After the feature works you hand off to Postfactoring, then Conclusion, all under continuous Verification. A and D list earlier or out-of-order phases, and C names operations, not phases. (Worked Example; Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: What are the three sub-activities of actualization, in order?

options.text: Locate, analyse, prefactor
Predict, measure, report
Implement, incorporate, propagate
Test, deploy, conclude

questions.explanation: Actualization = implement the new functionality, incorporate it into old code, and propagate secondary changes. A lists earlier phases, and D/B are unrelated activity sets. ([ActLab]; Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: What is the correct order of the seven-phase change process?

options.text: Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion
Initiation, Impact Analysis, Concept Location, Actualization, Prefactoring, Postfactoring, Conclusion
Initiation, Prefactoring, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion
Concept Location, Initiation, Impact Analysis, Actualization, Prefactoring, Conclusion, Postfactoring

questions.explanation: The order is Initiation -> Concept Location -> Impact Analysis -> Prefactoring -> Actualization -> Postfactoring -> Conclusion, all under Verification. D, B, and C swap the order of Concept Location, Impact Analysis, or Prefactoring. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: Which continuous activity wraps the entire change process?

options.text: Verification
Propagation
Incorporation
Concept Location

questions.explanation: Verification is the continuous activity wrapping all seven phases. Incorporation and propagation are parts of actualization, and Concept Location is a single early phase. (Actualization p.1)

questions.stem: Which mnemonic correctly expands the SOLID letters in order?

options.text: Single Responsibility, Observer, Liskov, Indirection, Dependency Inversion
SRP, OCP, Low Coupling, ISP, DIP
Substitutability, Openness, Low coupling, Indirection, Delegation
Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion

questions.explanation: SOLID expands to Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion. The others swap in GRASP terms (Low coupling, Indirection) or design patterns (Observer). (OOPrinciples p.3)

questions.stem: Which set lists the four characteristics of clean architecture together with the correct count of SOLID principles and GRASP patterns?

options.text: Independent of UI/DB only; 7 SOLID; 7 GRASP
Fast + small + documented + tested; 5 SOLID; 5 GRASP
Testable + independent of UI/DB/frameworks; 5 SOLID; 9 GRASP
Testable + independent of UI/DB/frameworks; 9 SOLID; 5 GRASP

questions.explanation: Clean architecture is testable and independent of UI/DB/frameworks; SOLID has 5 principles and GRASP has 9 patterns. D swaps the counts, B lists wrong characteristics, and A miscounts both. (Clean Architecture p.3; OOPrinciples p.3, p.18)

questions.stem: How do SOLID and Clean Architecture make actualization cheaper, in one sentence?

options.text: They lower coupling and put variation behind stable interfaces, so incorporating new code ripples less, propagation terminates sooner, and impact-analysis recall improves
They remove the need for testing and verification
They move all business rules into the database
They guarantee every change is a one-field edit

questions.explanation: SOLID/Clean Architecture lower coupling and wrap variation behind stable interfaces (OCP/DIP/Protected Variations, Dependency Rule), so new code ripples less, propagation ends sooner, and recall improves. D, B, and C contradict the lecture. (Exam Focus)

questions.stem: Which pairing of term to category is CORRECT?

options.text: Onion = design pattern; Singleton = architecture
Clean = design pattern; MVC = architecture
Clean = architecture; MVC = design pattern
Hexagonal = design pattern; Observer = architecture

questions.explanation: Clean is an architecture; MVC is a design pattern. The other options miscategorise architectures (Onion, Hexagonal) as patterns and patterns (Singleton, Observer) as architectures. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: Which metric measures 'of the classes that actually changed, what fraction did you predict'?

options.text: Recall
Cohesion
Accuracy
Precision

questions.explanation: Recall = TP/(TP+FN) measures the fraction of actually-changed classes you predicted. Precision is about predicted-changed classes, accuracy combines all correct predictions, and cohesion is a GRASP quality. (Actualization p.27)

questions.stem: Which metric measures 'of the classes you predicted would change, what fraction actually did'?

options.text: Recall
Precision
Coupling
Specificity

questions.explanation: Precision = TP/(TP+FP) measures the fraction of predicted-changed classes that actually changed. Recall is about actually-changed classes, specificity concerns true negatives, and coupling is a GRASP quality. (Actualization p.26)

questions.stem: Which confusion-matrix value is missing from the dangerous misses, and what made Ericsson's recall low?

options.text: True positives (TP=64) — too many correct hits
False positives (FP=64) — too many false alarms
True negatives (TN=64) — too many correct unchanged calls
False negatives (FN=64) — the many missed-but-changed classes drove recall down to 32%

questions.explanation: Recall was low because FN=64 (predicted unchanged but actually changed) is large; recall = 30/(30+64) = 32%. FP was 0 (not 64), and 64 is neither the TP nor the TN value. (Actualization p.25-27)

questions.stem: Which principle says 'depend upon abstractions, not concretions'?

options.text: Dependency Inversion Principle
Liskov Substitution Principle
Single Responsibility Principle
Interface Segregation Principle

questions.explanation: Depending on abstractions, not concretions, is the Dependency Inversion Principle. SRP is one reason to change, ISP is client-specific interfaces, and LSP is substitutability. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: Which principle says 'open for extension, closed for modification'?

options.text: Law of Demeter
Dependency Inversion Principle
Composite Reuse Principle
Open/Closed Principle

questions.explanation: 'Open for extension, closed for modification' is the Open/Closed Principle. DIP is about abstractions, CRP is composition over inheritance, and the Law of Demeter limits transitive calls. (OOPrinciples p.6)

questions.stem: Which principle is also known as the substitutability principle?

options.text: Open/Closed Principle
Single Responsibility Principle
Interface Segregation Principle
Liskov Substitution Principle

questions.explanation: The Liskov Substitution Principle is the substitutability principle. ISP, SRP, and OCP are distinct principles with different names. (OOPrinciples p.8)

questions.stem: Which statement about CRP is the correct, non-overstated reading?

options.text: Composition and inheritance are interchangeable in all cases
Always use inheritance as the primary reuse mechanism
Favor composition over inheritance, but inheritance is still correct for genuine is-a polymorphism
Never use inheritance under any circumstances

questions.explanation: CRP favors composition but allows inheritance for genuine is-a polymorphism (used in the Farm/Pig and OCP examples). D overstates it, B is the catastrophic mistake CRP warns against, and A ignores the tradeoffs. (OOPrinciples p.14)

questions.stem: Which mnemonic ordering matches the nine GRASP patterns as listed in the deck?

options.text: Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller, Polymorphism, Indirection, Pure Fabrication, Protected Variations
Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Information Expert, Creator, Controller, Polymorphism, Pure Fabrication, Indirection, Protected Variations
Creator, Information Expert, High Cohesion, Low Coupling, Polymorphism, Controller, Pure Fabrication, Indirection, Protected Variations
Information Expert, Creator, Controller, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Indirection, Polymorphism, Protected Variations, Pure Fabrication

questions.explanation: The deck lists them as Information Expert, Creator, Low Coupling, High Cohesion, Controller, Polymorphism, Indirection, Pure Fabrication, Protected Variations. The other options reorder the same nine items. (OOPrinciples p.18)

questions.stem: Which gotcha correctly states the Dependency Rule direction?

options.text: Dependencies may point any direction as long as the code compiles
Dependencies point inward toward stable abstractions; outer layers know inner layers, inner layers know nothing about outer layers
Dependencies point toward the database at the center
Dependencies point outward toward volatile details; inner layers know outer layers

questions.explanation: The Dependency Rule points dependencies inward toward stable abstractions; outer layers know inner ones, never the reverse. D is the classic reversal, A ignores the rule, and C puts a detail at the center. (Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: Which gotcha correctly distinguishes ISP from SRP?

options.text: SRP is about databases; ISP is about views
SRP is about interfaces; ISP is about classes
They are identical principles
SRP is a CLASS having one reason to change; ISP is INTERFACES being client-specific and cohesive

questions.explanation: SRP concerns a class's single reason to change; ISP concerns client-specific, cohesive interfaces — different scopes that rhyme. B swaps them, C conflates them, and A is unrelated. (OOPrinciples p.4, p.10)

questions.stem: Which item is a 'detail' / plug-in rather than the center of a clean architecture?

options.text: The application-specific business rules
The database
The enterprise business rules
The use-case interactor

questions.explanation: The database is a detail / plug-in, not the center. The enterprise and application business rules (entities and interactors) are the stable core that details plug into. (Clean Architecture p.16-17)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern most directly operationalises the Law of Demeter's 'beware transitive visibility'?

options.text: Creator
High Cohesion
Information Expert
Indirection

questions.explanation: GRASP Indirection explicitly says 'beware of transitive visibility', operationalising the Law of Demeter by inserting a mediating intermediary. Creator, High Cohesion, and Information Expert address other concerns. (OOPrinciples p.15, p.25)

questions.stem: Which chain of principles all reduce to the single idea 'wrap a predicted point of change behind a stable seam'?

options.text: Open/Closed, Dependency Inversion, GRASP Indirection, and GRASP Protected Variations
Single Responsibility, High Cohesion, Creator, and Information Expert
Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Low Coupling, and Controller
Composite Reuse, Law of Demeter, Pure Fabrication, and Polymorphism

questions.explanation: OCP, DIP, Indirection, and Protected Variations are all special cases of wrapping a predicted change point behind a stable seam (Protected Variations being the grand unifier). The other groupings address responsibility placement, substitutability, or reuse, not the seam idea. (OOPrinciples p.27; Clean Architecture p.8)

questions.stem: Which memory hook correctly orders the Clean Architecture layers AND the dependency direction?

options.text: Out-to-in: Entities, Use cases, Interface adapters, Frameworks; dependencies flow outward
Out-to-in: Use cases, Entities, Frameworks, Interface adapters; dependencies flow outward
Out-to-in: Frameworks, Interface adapters, Use cases, Entities ('FIUE'); dependencies flow the other way, inward
In-to-out: Frameworks, Use cases, Entities, Interface adapters; dependencies flow inward

questions.explanation: Out-to-in the layers are Frameworks, Interface adapters, Use cases, Entities ('FIUE'), but dependencies flow the other way — inward toward Entities. The other options scramble the layer order or reverse the dependency direction. (Memory hooks; Clean Architecture p.5-8)

questions.stem: What is the simplest of the three incorporation shapes?

options.text: Replacement of a class
Incorporating a new supplier
New responsibility is local (a self-contained leaf)
New responsibility is composite

questions.explanation: The 'new responsibility is local' shape — a self-contained leaf old code calls into — is the simplest. Composite brings a subtree, new-supplier is where propagation is most likely, and replacement reroutes references. (Actualization p.9)

questions.stem: In the small-change ZIP example, what was the field widened from and to?

options.text: From zip[5] to zip[9]
From zip[4] to zip[8]
From zip[5] to zip[10]
From zip[9] to zip[5]

questions.explanation: The Address ZIP field was widened from zip[5] (5-digit) to zip[9] (9-digit). The other options reverse or alter the sizes. (Actualization p.2-4)

questions.stem: What sound does the new Pig subclass make in the deck's polymorphism example?

options.text: Oink
Baa
Moo
Quack

questions.explanation: The Pig's makeSound() prints 'Oink'. Moo and Baa would be Cow and Sheep, and Quack is not in the example. (Actualization p.6-7)

questions.stem: What does the 'ripple effect' alternative name refer to in the deck?

options.text: The precision of an impact prediction
The innermost architecture layer
Change propagation spreading to other components of the system
A design pattern from the Gang of Four

questions.explanation: The ripple effect is the deck's alternative name for change propagation spreading to other components. The other options are unrelated concepts. (Actualization p.5)

questions.stem: In the Ericsson study, how many classes did the programmers correctly predict as changed (true positives)?
questions.explanation: True positives = 30 (predicted changed and actually changed). 64 = false negatives, 42 = true negatives, 136 = total. (Actualization p.25)

questions.stem: What does a false negative represent in impact analysis?

options.text: A class predicted unchanged that stayed unchanged
A class predicted unchanged that actually changed
A class predicted changed that stayed unchanged
A class predicted changed that actually changed

questions.explanation: A false negative is predicted-unchanged-but-actually-changed — a dangerous miss. D is a true positive, C is a false positive, and A is a true negative. (Actualization p.25)

questions.stem: Which layer is described as holding 'enterprise-wide business rules that are least likely to change'?

options.text: Entities
Interface Adapters
Frameworks and Drivers
The View

questions.explanation: Entities hold enterprise-wide business rules, least likely to change. Frameworks & Drivers and Interface Adapters are outer layers, and the View is the outermost UI. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: Which layer holds 'application-specific business rules' and is also called the interactors?

options.text: Use Cases
Frameworks and Drivers
Interface Adapters
Entities

questions.explanation: The Use Cases layer holds application-specific business rules and is also called interactors. Entities are application-independent, and the other two are outer layers. (Clean Architecture p.7)

questions.stem: What does the Presenter produce from the Response Model?

options.text: An Entity
A Request Model
A View Model
A stored procedure

questions.explanation: The Presenter translates the Response Model into a View Model. The Request Model is input, an Entity is a business object, and a stored procedure is forbidden for business rules. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: Which data structure carries primitive input from the delivery mechanism into the interactor?

options.text: The Entity Gateway
The Request Model
The Response Model
The View Model

questions.explanation: The Request Model is the plain primitive-typed structure carrying input into the interactor. The Response Model is the result, the View Model is display-ready, and the Entity Gateway is a persistence interface. (Clean Architecture p.10)

questions.stem: Which principle is violated when one fat interface forces implementers to carry methods they don't need?

options.text: Open/Closed Principle
Liskov Substitution Principle
Interface Segregation Principle
Single Responsibility Principle

questions.explanation: A fat interface forcing unneeded methods violates the Interface Segregation Principle. SRP is about class responsibilities, LSP about substitutability, and OCP about extension vs modification. (OOPrinciples p.10-11)

questions.stem: Which principle is violated when a class instantiates a concrete dependency with 'new' instead of depending on an abstraction?

options.text: Interface Segregation Principle
Single Responsibility Principle
Composite Reuse Principle
Dependency Inversion Principle

questions.explanation: Newing a concretion instead of depending on an abstraction violates the Dependency Inversion Principle. ISP, SRP, and CRP address interfaces, responsibilities, and reuse respectively. (OOPrinciples p.12-13)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern says 'put behaviour where the data lives'?

options.text: Creator
Indirection
Information Expert
Controller

questions.explanation: Information Expert assigns a responsibility to the class with the information to fulfill it — put behaviour where the data lives. Creator handles creation, Controller handles events, and Indirection decouples via an intermediary. (OOPrinciples p.19)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern uses a made-up class like PersistentStorage that is not a domain concept?

options.text: Information Expert
Pure Fabrication
Protected Variations
Creator

questions.explanation: Pure Fabrication invents a non-domain class (e.g. PersistentStorage) for cohesion/low-coupling/reuse. Information Expert and Creator use domain classes, and Protected Variations wraps unstable points. (OOPrinciples p.26)

questions.stem: Which GRASP pattern handles system events via a façade or use-case controller?

options.text: High Cohesion
Creator
Polymorphism
Controller

questions.explanation: Controller assigns system-event handling to a façade or use-case/session controller (never a UI class). Polymorphism handles type-varying behavior, High Cohesion is a quality goal, and Creator handles creation. (OOPrinciples p.23)

questions.stem: Which two GRASP patterns are 'quality goals' (evaluative principles) rather than concrete structural patterns?

options.text: Low Coupling and High Cohesion
Polymorphism and Indirection
Creator and Controller
Pure Fabrication and Protected Variations

questions.explanation: Low Coupling and High Cohesion are evaluative quality goals (assign responsibilities to keep coupling low / cohesion high). The other pairs are concrete structural/assignment patterns. (OOPrinciples p.21-22)

questions.stem: Why should the View be 'so stupid you don't have to test it'?

options.text: Because the View holds the business rules
Because the View implements the input boundary
Because all presentation logic is in the testable Presenter/View Model, leaving the View with no logic worth testing
Because the View talks to the database

questions.explanation: Keeping formatting in the Presenter leaves the View trivial, so there is nothing to test. The View must not hold business rules, implement the input boundary, or touch the database. (Clean Architecture p.15)

questions.stem: Which principle does the deck say 'tells us how we can adhere to OCP'?

options.text: The Composite Reuse Principle
The Law of Demeter
The Dependency Inversion Principle
The Single Responsibility Principle

questions.explanation: The deck states 'DIP tells us how we can adhere to OCP' — depending on abstractions keeps entities closed to modification but open to extension. SRP, the Law of Demeter, and CRP are different principles. (OOPrinciples p.12)

questions.stem: Per the deck, why is even removing code not free?

options.text: Deletion only affects the View
Deletion improves recall to 100%
Deletion propagates — all references to the deleted functionality must be deleted, causing secondary changes
Deletion always switches the database

questions.explanation: Deletion of obsolete functionality propagates: all references must be deleted, causing secondary changes elsewhere. The other options are false. (Actualization p.22)

questions.stem: What is the relationship between precision and recall in the Ericsson result?

options.text: Precision was perfect (100%) but recall was low (32%) — high precision is easy, high recall is hard
Precision was low (32%) but recall was perfect (100%)
Both precision and recall were 32%
Both precision and recall were 100%

questions.explanation: Ericsson had 100% precision (no false alarms) but only 32% recall (missed two-thirds of changed classes) — high precision is easy, high recall is hard. D, B, and C misstate the numbers. (Actualization p.26-27)

questions.stem: Hexagonal, Onion, and Clean are examples of what, and MVC, MVVM, MVP are examples of what?

options.text: Architectures; design patterns
Design patterns; architectures
Both are architectures
Both are design patterns

questions.explanation: Hexagonal/Onion/Clean are ARCHITECTURES; MVC/MVVM/MVP are DESIGN PATTERNS. B swaps them, and C/D collapse the distinction the deck draws. (Clean Architecture p.2)

questions.stem: Under GRASP Creator, who should create a SaleLineItem if a Sale aggregates SaleLineItems?

options.text: A made-up PersistentStorage class
Whichever class has the fewest fields
The window/view class
The Sale, because it aggregates the SaleLineItems

questions.explanation: Creator assigns creation to the class that aggregates/contains the created object — here the Sale aggregates SaleLineItems, so Sale creates them. The view is excluded by Controller, PersistentStorage is Pure Fabrication, and field count is irrelevant. (OOPrinciples p.20)

questions.stem: Which best states the maintenance benefit of SOLID and GRASP together?

options.text: They guarantee that no class ever needs to change again
Lower coupling and higher cohesion mean changes touch fewer classes, so propagation is shorter and the design is easier to maintain
They move business logic into stored procedures
They eliminate the need for impact analysis

questions.explanation: SOLID/GRASP lower coupling and raise cohesion, so changes touch fewer classes and propagation is shorter — easier maintenance. A and D overstate it, and C contradicts the database-is-a-detail rule. (OOPrinciples p.21-22; Exam Focus)

questions.stem: What is the Repository pattern called in the Clean Architecture deck's Entity Gateway discussion?

options.text: The Delivery Mechanism
The Request Model
The Entity Gateway (interface declared with business rules, implemented in the detail layer)
The Presenter

questions.explanation: The Repository is the Entity Gateway: an interface declared with the business rules and implemented in the detail layer to reach the Database API. The Presenter, Request Model, and Delivery Mechanism are data-flow components. (Clean Architecture p.7, p.18)

questions.stem: What triggers the start of change propagation (ripple effects)?

options.text: Concept Location finding the starting class
Computing precision and recall
Incorporation — the point at which new code is plugged into the existing code
Writing the README file

questions.explanation: Ripple effects begin at incorporation, when the new code is plugged in. Concept Location is an earlier phase, precision/recall is post-propagation scoring, and the README is unrelated. (Actualization p.5)
